25 million (2021)[[1]](#cite_note-e19-1) Total ([L1](/wiki/First_language "First language") plus [L2](/wiki/Second_language "Second language") speakers): 30 million (2021)[[2]](#cite_note-Eurobarometer_Languages-2)[[3]](#cite_note-UofL-3)

[Indo-European](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages")  * [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages")   + [West Germanic](/wiki/West_Germanic_languages "West Germanic languages")     - [Weser–Rhine Germanic](/wiki/Weser%E2%80%93Rhine_Germanic "Weser–Rhine Germanic")       * [Low Franconian](/wiki/Low_Franconian "Low Franconian")         + **Dutch**

[Frankish](/wiki/Frankish_language "Frankish language")  * [Old Dutch](/wiki/Old_Dutch "Old Dutch")   + [Middle Dutch](/wiki/Middle_Dutch "Middle Dutch")     - [Modern Dutch](/wiki/Modern_Dutch "Modern Dutch")

* [Central](/wiki/Central_Dutch_dialects "Central Dutch dialects") * [Flemish](/wiki/Flemish_dialects "Flemish dialects") * [Stadsfries](/wiki/Stadsfries_dialects "Stadsfries dialects") * [West Frisian Dutch](/wiki/West_Frisian_Dutch "West Frisian Dutch") * [Surinamese Dutch](/wiki/Surinamese_Dutch "Surinamese Dutch")

* [Latin](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script") ([Dutch alphabet](/wiki/Dutch_alphabet "Dutch alphabet")) * [Dutch six-dot Braille](/wiki/Dutch_six-dot_Braille "Dutch six-dot Braille") * [Dutch eight-dot Braille](/wiki/Dutch_eight-dot_Braille "Dutch eight-dot Braille")

* [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands")   * [Aruba](/wiki/Aruba "Aruba") * [Bonaire](/wiki/Bonaire "Bonaire") * [Curaçao](/wiki/Cura%C3%A7ao "Curaçao") * [Sint Eustatius](/wiki/Sint_Eustatius "Sint Eustatius") * [Sint Maarten](/wiki/Sint_Maarten "Sint Maarten") * [Saba](/wiki/Saba_(island) "Saba (island)")   * [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") * [Suriname](/wiki/Suriname "Suriname")  [Organisations](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_Afrikaans_or_Dutch_are_official_languages "List of countries and territories where Afrikaans or Dutch are official languages")   * [Benelux](/wiki/Benelux "Benelux") * [Caribbean Community](/wiki/Caribbean_Community "Caribbean Community") * [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") * [PROSUR](/wiki/Forum_for_the_Progress_and_Integration_of_South_America "Forum for the Progress and Integration of South America") * [South American Union](/wiki/Union_of_South_American_Nations "Union of South American Nations")

infobox.value: Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:IPA#Rendering_issues "Help:IPA"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)") instead of [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") characters. For an introductory guide on IPA symbols, see [Help:IPA](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA"). | |

([endonym](/wiki/Endonym "Endonym"): *Nederlands* [[ˈneːdərlɑnts]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-Nederlands.ogg "File:Nl-Nederlands.ogg"), *Nederlandse taal*) is a [West Germanic language](/wiki/West_Germanic_languages "West Germanic languages") of the [Indo-European language family](/wiki/Indo-European_language_family "Indo-European language family"), spoken by about 25 million people as a first language[[4]](#cite_note-Feiten-4) and 5 million as a second language and is the [third most spoken](/wiki/List_of_languages_by_total_number_of_speakers "List of languages by total number of speakers") Germanic language. In Europe, Dutch is the native language of most of the population of the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") and [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") (which includes 60% of the population of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium")).[[2]](#cite_note-Eurobarometer_Languages-2)[[3]](#cite_note-UofL-3) Dutch was one of the official languages of [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") until 1925, when it was replaced by [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans"), a separate but partially [mutually intelligible](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") [daughter language](/wiki/Daughter_language "Daughter language")[[5]](#cite_note-5) of Dutch.[[a]](#cite_note-6) Afrikaans, depending on the definition used, may be considered a [sister language](/wiki/Sister_language "Sister language"),[[6]](#cite_note-7) spoken, to some degree, by at least 16 million people, mainly in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") and [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia "Namibia"),[[b]](#cite_note-8) and evolving from [Cape Dutch](/wiki/Cape_Dutch "Cape Dutch") dialects.

A young woman speaking Dutch (1:32)

**Dutch** ([endonym](/wiki/Endonym "Endonym"): *Nederlands* [[ˈneːdərlɑnts]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-Nederlands.ogg "File:Nl-Nederlands.ogg"), *Nederlandse taal*) is a [West Germanic language](/wiki/West_Germanic_languages "West Germanic languages") of the [Indo-European language family](/wiki/Indo-European_language_family "Indo-European language family"), spoken by about 25 million people as a first language[[4]](#cite_note-Feiten-4) and 5 million as a second language and is the [third most spoken](/wiki/List_of_languages_by_total_number_of_speakers "List of languages by total number of speakers") Germanic language. In Europe, Dutch is the native language of most of the population of the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") and [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") (which includes 60% of the population of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium")).[[2]](#cite_note-Eurobarometer_Languages-2)[[3]](#cite_note-UofL-3) Dutch was one of the official languages of [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") until 1925, when it was replaced by [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans"), a separate but partially [mutually intelligible](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") [daughter language](/wiki/Daughter_language "Daughter language")[[5]](#cite_note-5) of Dutch.[[a]](#cite_note-6) Afrikaans, depending on the definition used, may be considered a [sister language](/wiki/Sister_language "Sister language"),[[6]](#cite_note-7) spoken, to some degree, by at least 16 million people, mainly in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") and [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia "Namibia"),[[b]](#cite_note-8) and evolving from [Cape Dutch](/wiki/Cape_Dutch "Cape Dutch") dialects.

In South America, Dutch is the native language of the majority of the population of [Suriname](/wiki/Suriname "Suriname"), and spoken as a second or third language in the [multilingual](/wiki/Multilingualism "Multilingualism") [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") island countries of [Aruba](/wiki/Aruba "Aruba"), [Curaçao](/wiki/Cura%C3%A7ao "Curaçao") and [Sint Maarten](/wiki/Sint_Maarten "Sint Maarten"). All these countries have recognised Dutch as one of their official languages, and are involved in one way or another in the [Dutch Language Union](/wiki/Dutch_Language_Union "Dutch Language Union").[[7]](#cite_note-9) The [Dutch Caribbean](/wiki/Dutch_Caribbean "Dutch Caribbean") municipalities ([St. Eustatius](/wiki/Sint_Eustatius "Sint Eustatius"), [Saba](/wiki/Saba_(island) "Saba (island)") and [Bonaire](/wiki/Bonaire "Bonaire")) have Dutch as one of the official languages.[[8]](#cite_note-10) In Asia, Dutch was used in the [Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies") (now mostly [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia")) by a limited educated elite of around 2% of the total population, including over 1 million indigenous Indonesians,[[9]](#cite_note-11) until it was banned in 1957, but the ban was lifted afterwards.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaier2005-12) About a fifth of the [Indonesian language](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language") can be traced to Dutch, including many [loan words](/wiki/List_of_Dutch_loanwords_in_Indonesian "List of Dutch loanwords in Indonesian").[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaier2005-12) Indonesia's [Civil Code](/wiki/Civil_Code_of_Indonesia "Civil Code of Indonesia") has not been officially translated, and the original Dutch language version dating from colonial times remains the authoritative version.[[11]](#cite_note-13) Up to half a million native speakers reside in the United States, Canada and Australia combined,[[c]](#cite_note-14) and historical linguistic minorities on the verge of extinction remain in parts of France[[12]](#cite_note-15) and Germany.[[d]](#cite_note-16)

Dutch is one of the closest relatives of both [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") and English,[[e]](#cite_note-17) and is colloquially said to be "roughly in between" them.[[f]](#cite_note-18) Dutch, like English, has not undergone the [High German consonant shift](/wiki/High_German_consonant_shift "High German consonant shift"), does not use [Germanic umlaut](/wiki/Germanic_umlaut "Germanic umlaut") as a grammatical marker, has largely abandoned the use of the [subjunctive](/wiki/Subjunctive_in_Dutch "Subjunctive in Dutch"), and has [levelled](/wiki/Morphological_leveling "Morphological leveling") much of its [morphology](/wiki/Morphology_(linguistics) "Morphology (linguistics)"), including most of its [case system](/wiki/Case_system "Case system").[[g]](#cite_note-19) Features shared with German, however, include the survival of two to three [grammatical genders](/wiki/Gender_in_Dutch_grammar "Gender in Dutch grammar") – albeit with few grammatical consequences[[h]](#cite_note-20) – as well as the use of [modal particles](/wiki/Modal_particle "Modal particle"),[[13]](#cite_note-21) [final-obstruent devoicing](/wiki/Final-obstruent_devoicing "Final-obstruent devoicing"), and (similar) [word order](/wiki/V2_word_order#Dutch_and_Afrikaans "V2 word order").[[i]](#cite_note-22) Dutch vocabulary is mostly Germanic; it incorporates slightly more [Romance loans](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") than German, but far fewer than English.[[j]](#cite_note-23)

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name")]

Main article: [Terminology of the Low Countries](/wiki/Terminology_of_the_Low_Countries "Terminology of the Low Countries")

In Belgium, the Netherlands and Suriname, the native official name for Dutch is *Nederlands*[[14]](#cite_note-24)[[15]](#cite_note-25) (historically *Nederlandsch* before the [Dutch orthographic reforms](/wiki/History_of_Dutch_orthography "History of Dutch orthography")).[[16]](#cite_note-26) Sometimes *Vlaams* ("[Flemish](/wiki/Flemish_dialects "Flemish dialects")") is used as well to describe [Standard Dutch](/wiki/Standard_Dutch "Standard Dutch") in [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders"), whereas *Hollands* ("[Hollandic](/wiki/Hollandic "Hollandic")") is occasionally used as a colloquial term for the standard language in the central and northwestern parts of the Netherlands.[[17]](#cite_note-27)

English uses the adjective *Dutch* as a noun for the language of the Netherlands and Flanders. The word is derived from [Proto-Germanic](/wiki/Proto-Germanic_language "Proto-Germanic language") *[\*þiudiskaz](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Reconstruction:Proto-Germanic/%C3%BEiudiskaz#Proto-Germanic "wikt:Reconstruction:Proto-Germanic/þiudiskaz")*. The stem of this word, *[\*þeudō](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Reconstruction:Proto-Germanic/%C3%BEeud%C5%8D#Proto-Germanic "wikt:Reconstruction:Proto-Germanic/þeudō")*, meant "people" in Proto-Germanic, and *[\*-iskaz](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Reconstruction:Proto-Germanic/-iskaz#Proto-Germanic "wikt:Reconstruction:Proto-Germanic/-iskaz")* was an adjective-forming suffix, of which *[-ish](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-ish#English "wikt:-ish")* is the [Modern English](/wiki/Modern_English "Modern English") form.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMalloryAdams2006269-28) **Theodiscus** was its [Latinised](/wiki/List_of_Latinised_names "List of Latinised names") form[[19]](#cite_note-29) and used as an [adjective](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective") referring to the [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages") [vernaculars](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") of the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"). In this sense, it meant "the language of the common people". The term was used as opposed to [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"), the *non*-native language of writing and the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").[[20]](#cite_note-30) It was first recorded in 786, when the [Bishop of Ostia](/wiki/Suburbicarian_Diocese_of_Ostia "Suburbicarian Diocese of Ostia") writes to [Pope Adrian I](/wiki/Pope_Adrian_I "Pope Adrian I") about a [synod](/wiki/Synod "Synod") taking place in [Corbridge](/wiki/Corbridge "Corbridge"), [England](/wiki/England "England"), where the decisions are being written down "*tam Latine quam theodisce*" meaning "in Latin as well as common vernacular".[[21]](#cite_note-31)[[22]](#cite_note-32)[[23]](#cite_note-33)

According to a hypothesis by De Grauwe, In northern [West Francia](/wiki/West_Francia "West Francia") (i.e. modern-day Belgium) the term would take on a new meaning during the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"), when, within the context of a highly dichromatic linguistic landscape, it came to be the [antonym](/wiki/Antonym "Antonym") of *[\*walhisk](/wiki/Walhaz "Walhaz")* (Romance-speakers, specifically [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French")).[[24]](#cite_note-34) The word, now rendered as *dietsc* (Southwestern variant) or *duutsc* (Central and Northern Variant), could refer to the Dutch language itself, as well as a broader Germanic category depending on context. During the [High Middle Ages](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages") "*Dietsc*/*Duutsc*" was increasingly used as an umbrella term for the specific Germanic dialects spoken in the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries"), its meaning being largely implicitly provided by the regional orientation of medieval Dutch society: apart from the higher echelons of the clergy and nobility, mobility was largely static and hence while "Dutch" could by extension also be used in its earlier sense, referring to what today would be called Germanic dialects as opposed to [Romance dialects](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages"), in many cases it was understood or meant to refer to the language now known as Dutch.[[25]](#cite_note-35)

In the Low Countries *Dietsch* or its early modern Dutch form *Duytsch* as an endonym for Dutch gradually went out of common use and was gradually replaced by the Dutch endonym *Nederlands*. This designation (first attested in 1482) started at the [Burgundian court](/wiki/Burgundian_Netherlands "Burgundian Netherlands") in the 15th century, although the use of *neder*, *laag*, *bas*, and *inferior* ("nether" or "low") to refer to the area known as the Low Countries goes back further in time, with the [Romans](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") referring to the region as *[Germania Inferior](/wiki/Germania_Inferior "Germania Inferior")* ("Lower" Germania).[[26]](#cite_note-36)[[27]](#cite_note-37)[[28]](#cite_note-38) It is a reference to the Low Countries' downriver location at the [Rhine–Meuse–Scheldt delta](/wiki/Rhine%E2%80%93Meuse%E2%80%93Scheldt_delta "Rhine–Meuse–Scheldt delta") near the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea").

From 1551, the designation *Nederlands* received strong competition from the name *Nederduytsch* (literally "Low Dutch", *Dutch* being used in its archaic sense covering all continental West Germanic languages). It is a [calque](/wiki/Calque "Calque") of the aforementioned Roman province *Germania Inferior* and an attempt by early Dutch grammarians to give their language more prestige by linking it to Roman times. Likewise, *Hoogduits* ("High German") and *Overlands* ("Upper-landish") came into use as a Dutch exonym for the various German dialects used in neighboring German states.[[29]](#cite_note-39) Use of *Nederduytsch* was popular in the 16th century but ultimately lost out over *Nederlands* during the close of the 18th century, with *(Hoog)Duytsch* establishing itself as the Dutch exonym for German during this same period.

In the 19th century Germany saw the rise of the categorisation of dialects, with German [dialectologists](/wiki/Dialectology "Dialectology") terming the German dialects spoken in the mountainous south of Germany as *Hochdeutsch* ("High German"). Subsequently, German dialects spoken in the north were designated as *Niederdeutsch* ("Low German"). The names for these dialects were calqued by Dutch linguists as *Nederduits* and *Hoogduits*. As a result, *Nederduits* no longer serves as a synonym for the Dutch language. In the 19th century, the term "*Diets*" was revived by Dutch linguists and historians as well, as a poetic name for [Middle Dutch](/wiki/Middle_Dutch "Middle Dutch") and [its literature](/wiki/Middle_Dutch_literature "Middle Dutch literature").[[30]](#cite_note-40)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/GERMANICEXPANSION.GIF/250px-GERMANICEXPANSION.GIF)](/wiki/File:GERMANICEXPANSION.GIF)

Map of the [pre-Roman Iron Age in Northern Europe](/wiki/Pre-Roman_Iron_Age_in_Northern_Europe "Pre-Roman Iron Age in Northern Europe") culture(s) associated with the [Proto-Germanic language](/wiki/Proto-Germanic_language "Proto-Germanic language"), ca 500–50 BCE. The area south of Scandinavia is the [Jastorf culture](/wiki/Jastorf_culture "Jastorf culture").

[Old Dutch](/wiki/Old_Dutch "Old Dutch") can be discerned more or less around the same time as [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") (Anglo-Saxon), [Old High German](/wiki/Old_High_German "Old High German"), [Old Frisian](/wiki/Old_Frisian "Old Frisian"), and [Old Saxon](/wiki/Old_Saxon "Old Saxon"). These names are derived from the modern [standard languages](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language"). In this age no standard languages had yet developed, while a perfect West Germanic [dialect continuum](/wiki/Dialect_continuum "Dialect continuum") remained present; the division reflects the contingent future contribution dialect groups would have to the later languages. The early form of Dutch was a set of Franconian dialects spoken by the [Salian Franks](/wiki/Salian_Franks "Salian Franks") in the 5th century. These happened to develop through [Middle Dutch](/wiki/Middle_Dutch "Middle Dutch") to Modern Dutch over the course of fifteen centuries.[[31]](#cite_note-41) During that period, they forced Old Frisian back from the western coast to the north of the Low Countries, and influenced or even replaced Old Saxon spoken in the east (contiguous with the Low German area). On the other hand, Dutch has been replaced in adjacent lands in present-day France and Germany. The division into Old, Middle and Modern Dutch is mostly conventional, since the transition between them was very gradual. One of the few moments when [linguists](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics") can detect something of a revolution is when the Dutch standard language emerged and quickly established itself. The development of the Dutch language is illustrated by the following sentence in Old, Middle and Modern Dutch:

* *Irlôsin sol an frithe sêla mîna fan thên thia ginâcont mi, wanda under managon he was mit mi* (Old Dutch)
* *Erlossen sal [hi] in vrede siele mine van dien die genaken mi, want onder menegen hi was met mi* (Middle Dutch)
* *Verlossen zal hij in vrede ziel mijn van degenen die genaken mij, want onder menigen hij was met mij* (Modern Dutch, same word order)
* *Hij zal mijn ziel in vrede verlossen van degenen die mij genaken, want onder menigen was hij met mij* (Modern Dutch, default word order)[[32]](#cite_note-42)
* *He will deliver my soul in peace from those who approach me, because, amongst many, he was with me* (English)[[33]](#cite_note-43)

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Origins")]

Main article: [History of the Dutch language](/wiki/History_of_the_Dutch_language "History of the Dutch language")

Among the [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages"), Dutch is grouped within the [Germanic languages](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages"), meaning it shares a [common ancestor](/wiki/Common_ancestor "Common ancestor") with languages such as English, German, and the [Scandinavian languages](/wiki/Scandinavian_languages "Scandinavian languages"). All Germanic languages are subject to the [Grimm's law](/wiki/Grimm%27s_law "Grimm's law") and [Verner's law](/wiki/Verner%27s_law "Verner's law") sound shifts, which originated in the [Proto-Germanic language](/wiki/Proto-Germanic_language "Proto-Germanic language") and define the basic features differentiating them from other Indo-European languages. This is assumed to have taken place in approximately the mid-first millennium BCE in the [pre-Roman Northern European Iron Age](/wiki/Pre-Roman_Iron_Age_in_Northern_Europe "Pre-Roman Iron Age in Northern Europe").[[34]](#cite_note-44)

The Germanic languages are traditionally divided into three groups: [East](/wiki/East_Germanic_languages "East Germanic languages") (now extinct), [West](/wiki/West_Germanic_languages "West Germanic languages"), and [North](/wiki/North_Germanic_languages "North Germanic languages") Germanic.[[35]](#cite_note-45) They remained mutually intelligible throughout the [Migration Period](/wiki/Migration_Period "Migration Period"). Dutch is part of the West Germanic group, which also includes English, [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language"), [Frisian](/wiki/Frisian_languages "Frisian languages"), [Low German](/wiki/Low_German "Low German") (Old Saxon) and [High German](/wiki/High_German_languages "High German languages"). It is characterised by a number of [phonological](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology") and [morphological](/wiki/Morphology_(linguistics) "Morphology (linguistics)") innovations not found in North or East Germanic.[[36]](#cite_note-46)

### Frankish (3rd–5th centuries)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Frankish (3rd–5th centuries)")]

Main article: [Frankish language](/wiki/Frankish_language "Frankish language")

The Frankish language itself is not directly attested, the only possible exception being the [Bergakker inscription](/wiki/Bergakker_inscription "Bergakker inscription"), found near the Dutch city of [Tiel](/wiki/Tiel "Tiel"), which may represent a primary record of 5th-century Frankish. Although some place names recorded in Roman texts such as **vadam** (modern Dutch: **wad**, English: "mudflat"), could arguably be considered as the oldest single "Dutch" words, the Bergakker inscription yields the oldest evidence of Dutch morphology. However, interpretations of the rest of the text lack consensus.[[37]](#cite_note-47)

The [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks") emerged in the southern Netherlands ([Salian Franks](/wiki/Salian_Franks "Salian Franks")) and central Germany ([Ripuarian Franks](/wiki/Ripuarian_Franks "Ripuarian Franks")), and later descended into [Gaul](/wiki/Gaul "Gaul"). The name of [their kingdom](/wiki/Francia "Francia") survives in that of France. Although they ruled the [Gallo-Romans](/wiki/Gallo-Roman_culture "Gallo-Roman culture") for nearly 300 years, their language, [Frankish](/wiki/Frankish_language "Frankish language"), became extinct in most of France and was replaced by later forms of the language throughout Luxembourg and Germany in around the 7th century. It was replaced in France by [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") (a [Romance language](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") with a considerable Old Frankish influence).

However, the Old Franconian language did not die out at large, as it continued to be spoken in the Low Countries, and subsequently evolved into what is now called Old Low Franconian or Old Dutch in the Low Countries. In fact, Old Frankish could be reconstructed from Old Dutch and Frankish loanwords in Old French.[[38]](#cite_note-48)

### Old Dutch (5th–12th centuries)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Old Dutch (5th–12th centuries)")]

Main article: [Old Dutch](/wiki/Old_Dutch "Old Dutch")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/OldDutcharea.png/250px-OldDutcharea.png)](/wiki/File:OldDutcharea.png)

Area in which Old Dutch was spoken

The term *Old Dutch* or *Old Low Franconian*[[39]](#cite_note-49)[[40]](#cite_note-50) refers to the set of [Franconian dialects](/wiki/Franconian_(linguistics) "Franconian (linguistics)") (i.e. West Germanic varieties that are assumed to have evolved from [Frankish](/wiki/Frankish_language "Frankish language")) spoken in the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries") during the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"), from around the 5th to the 12th century.[[41]](#cite_note-51) Old Dutch is mostly recorded on fragmentary relics, and words have been reconstructed from Middle Dutch and Old Dutch loanwords in French.[[42]](#cite_note-Webster-52) Old Dutch is regarded as the primary stage in the development of a separate Dutch language. It was spoken by the descendants of the [Salian Franks](/wiki/Salian_Franks "Salian Franks") who occupied what is now the southern [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), northern [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), part of northern France, and parts of the [Lower Rhine](/wiki/Lower_Rhine "Lower Rhine") regions of Germany.

The High German consonant shift, moving over Western Europe from south to west, caused a differentiation with the [Central and High Franconian](/wiki/High_Franconian_German "High Franconian German") in Germany. The latter would as a consequence evolve (along with [Alemannic](/wiki/Alemannic_German "Alemannic German"), [Bavarian](/wiki/Bavarian_language "Bavarian language") and [Lombardic](/wiki/Lombardic_language "Lombardic language")) into Old High German. At more or less the same time the Ingvaeonic nasal spirant law, moving over Western Europe from west to east, led to the development of [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") (or Anglo-Saxon), [Old Frisian](/wiki/Old_Frisian "Old Frisian") and [Old Saxon](/wiki/Old_Saxon "Old Saxon"). Hardly influenced by either development, Old Dutch probably remained relatively close to the original language of the Franks. However, the language did experience developments of its own, such as very early [final-obstruent devoicing](/wiki/Final-obstruent_devoicing "Final-obstruent devoicing"). In fact, the find at Bergakker indicates that the language may already have experienced this shift during the Old Frankish period.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Gallee_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Gallee_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gallee_(cropped).jpg)

The Utrecht baptismal vow

Attestations of Old Dutch sentences are extremely rare. The language is mostly recorded on fragmentary relics, and words have been reconstructed from Middle Dutch and loan words from Old Dutch in other languages.[[42]](#cite_note-Webster-52) The oldest recorded is found in the [Salic law](/wiki/Salic_law "Salic law"). In this [Frankish](/wiki/Franks "Franks") document written around 510 the oldest Dutch sentence has been identified: *Maltho thi afrio lito* ("I say to you, I free you, serf") used to free a serf. Another old fragment of Dutch is *Visc flot aftar themo uuatare* ("A fish was swimming in the water"). The oldest conserved larger Dutch text is the [Utrecht baptismal vow](/wiki/Old_Saxon_Baptismal_Vow "Old Saxon Baptismal Vow") (776–800) starting with *Forsachistu diobolae ... ec forsacho diabolae* (litt.: "Forsake you the devil? ... I forsake the devil"). If only for its poetic content, the most famous Old Dutch sentence is probably *[Hebban olla vogala](/wiki/Hebban_olla_vogala "Hebban olla vogala") nestas hagunnan, hinase hic enda tu, wat unbidan we nu* ("All birds have started making nests, except me and you, what are we waiting for"), is dated to around the year 1100, written by a Flemish [monk](/wiki/Monk "Monk") in a convent in [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_Kent "Rochester, Kent"), [England](/wiki/England "England"). Since the sentence speaks to the imagination, it is often erroneously stated as the oldest Dutch sentence.

### Middle Dutch (12th–15th centuries)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Middle Dutch (12th–15th centuries)")]

Main article: [Middle Dutch](/wiki/Middle_Dutch "Middle Dutch")

Old Dutch naturally evolved into [Middle Dutch](/wiki/Middle_Dutch "Middle Dutch"). The year 1150 is often chosen as the time of the transition, but it actually marks a time of profuse Dutch writing; during this period a rich [Medieval Dutch literature](/wiki/Medieval_Dutch_literature "Medieval Dutch literature") developed. There was at that time no overarching [standard language](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language"); Middle Dutch is rather a collective name for a number of closely related, mutually intelligible dialects spoken in the former Old Dutch area. Where Old Dutch fragments are very hard to read for untrained Modern Dutch speakers, the various literary works of Middle Dutch are somewhat more accessible.[[43]](#cite_note-53) The most notable difference between Old and Middle Dutch is in a feature of speech known as [vowel reduction](/wiki/Vowel_reduction "Vowel reduction"), whereby vowels in unstressed syllables are leveled to a [schwa](/wiki/Schwa "Schwa").

The Middle Dutch dialect areas were affected by political boundaries. The sphere of political influence of a certain ruler often also created a sphere of linguistic influence, with the language within the area becoming more homogenous. Following the contemporary political divisions they are in order of importance:

* [West Flemish](/wiki/West_Flemish "West Flemish") with the [County of Flanders](/wiki/County_of_Flanders "County of Flanders") at its centre. It had been influential during the earlier Middle Ages (the "Flemish expansion") but lost prestige to the neighbouring Brabantian in the 13th century.
* [Brabantian](/wiki/Brabantian_dialect "Brabantian dialect") (and related East Flemish), spoken primarily in the [Duchy of Brabant](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant") and adjacent parts. It was an influential dialect during most of the Middle Ages, during the so-called "Brabantian expansion" in which the influence of Brabant was extended outwards into other areas.
* [Hollandic](/wiki/Hollandic_dialect "Hollandic dialect"), which had the [County of Holland](/wiki/County_of_Holland "County of Holland") as its heartland, where originally [Old Frisian](/wiki/Old_Frisian "Old Frisian") was spoken. The people adopted Low Franconian[[44]](#cite_note-54)[[45]](#cite_note-55) and a new Frankish dialect with a Frisian [substrate](/wiki/Stratum_(linguistics) "Stratum (linguistics)") developed. It was less influential during most of the Middle Ages but became more so in the 16th century during the "Hollandic expansion"; the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War") took place in the Southern Netherlands during this period.
* [Limburgish](/wiki/Limburgish_language "Limburgish language"), spoken by the people in the modern-day provinces of [Dutch](/wiki/Limburg_(Netherlands) "Limburg (Netherlands)") and [Belgian Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Belgium) "Limburg (Belgium)"), and adjacent lands in Germany. It was over time tied to different political areas and is therefore the most divergent of the dialects. It was even partly influenced by the High German consonant shift and is the most distant to the later developed standard language to which it contributed little. It was, however, the earliest Middle Dutch dialect that developed a literary tradition.
* Since it is part of the Old Saxon and not Low Franconian (Old Dutch) area, [Dutch Low Saxon](/wiki/Dutch_Low_Saxon "Dutch Low Saxon") is not strictly a Dutch dialect. However, it was influenced by Middle Dutch since the 14th century and it did play a part in the formation of the standard Dutch language in later periods. It was spoken in the [Oversticht](/wiki/Overijssel#Oversticht "Overijssel") territories of the [episcopal principality of Utrecht](/wiki/Episcopal_principality_of_Utrecht "Episcopal principality of Utrecht") and adjacent parts of [Guelders](/wiki/Guelders "Guelders"). A [dialect continuum](/wiki/Dialect_continuum "Dialect continuum") remained present with Low Franconian areas to the west and Low Saxon areas to the east.

### Modern Dutch (15th century–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Modern Dutch (15th century–present)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Statenvertaling_title_page.jpg/250px-Statenvertaling_title_page.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statenvertaling_title_page.jpg)

Title page of the [Statenvertaling](/wiki/Statenvertaling "Statenvertaling") (1637) reads: *Biblia ... Uyt de Oorspronckelijcke talen in onse Neder-landtsche tale getrouwelijck over-geset.* (English: From the Original languages into our Dutch language faithfully translated.)[[46]](#cite_note-56)

A process of [standardisation](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language") started in the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), especially under the influence of the [Burgundian](/wiki/Burgundian_Netherlands "Burgundian Netherlands") Ducal Court in Dijon ([Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") after 1477). The dialects of Flanders and Brabant were the most influential around this time. The process of standardisation became much stronger at the start of the 16th century, mainly based on the urban dialect of [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"). The 1585 [fall of Antwerp](/wiki/Fall_of_Antwerp "Fall of Antwerp") to the Spanish army led to a flight to the northern Netherlands, where the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") declared its independence from Spain. This influenced the urban dialects of the province of [Holland](/wiki/County_of_Holland "County of Holland"). In 1637, a further important step was made towards a unified language,[[47]](#cite_note-57) when the [Statenvertaling](/wiki/Statenvertaling "Statenvertaling"), the first major Bible translation into Dutch, was created that people from all over the new republic could understand. It used elements from various, even [Dutch Low Saxon](/wiki/Dutch_Low_Saxon "Dutch Low Saxon"), dialects but was predominantly based on the urban dialects of Holland of post 16th century.[[48]](#cite_note-58)

In the [Southern Netherlands](/wiki/Southern_Netherlands "Southern Netherlands") (now Belgium and Luxembourg), developments were different. Under subsequent [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_Netherlands "Spanish Netherlands"), [Austrian](/wiki/Austrian_Netherlands "Austrian Netherlands") and [French rule](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars"), the standardisation of Dutch language came to a standstill. The state, law, and increasingly education used French, yet more than half the Belgian population were speaking a variety of Dutch. In the course of the 19th century, the [Flemish Movement](/wiki/Flemish_Movement "Flemish Movement") stood up for the rights of Dutch speakers, mostly referred to as "Flemish". However, the dialect variation was a serious disadvantage in the face of the standardised [francophony](/wiki/French_language "French language").[[49]](#cite_note-59) Since standardisation is a lengthy process, Dutch-speaking Belgium associated itself with the standard language that had already developed in the Netherlands over the centuries. Therefore, the situation in Belgium is essentially no different from that in the Netherlands, although there are recognisable differences in pronunciation, comparable to the pronunciation differences between standard British and standard American English.[[50]](#cite_note-60) In 1980 the Netherlands and Belgium concluded the [Language Union Treaty](/wiki/Dutch_Language_Union "Dutch Language Union"). This treaty lays down the principle that the two countries must gear their language policy to each other, among other things, for a common system of spelling.

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Classification")]

* [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages")
  + [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages")
    - [West Germanic](/wiki/West_Germanic_languages "West Germanic languages")
      * [Low Franconian](/wiki/Low_Franconian_languages "Low Franconian languages")
        + **Dutch**
          - Afrikaans, [Dutch-based creoles](/wiki/Dutch-based_creole_languages "Dutch-based creole languages")

Dutch belongs to its own [West Germanic](/wiki/West_Germanic_languages "West Germanic languages") sub-group, the [Low Franconian](/wiki/Low_Franconian "Low Franconian") languages, paired with its sister language Limburgish or East Low Franconian. Its closest relative is the [mutually intelligible](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") daughter language Afrikaans. Other West Germanic languages related to Dutch are [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and the un-standardised languages [Low German](/wiki/Low_German "Low German") and [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish").

Dutch stands out in combining some [Ingvaeonic characteristics](/wiki/Ingvaeonic_nasal_spirant_law "Ingvaeonic nasal spirant law") (occurring consistently in English and Frisian and reduced in intensity from west to east over the [continental West Germanic](/wiki/Continental_West_Germanic "Continental West Germanic") plane) with dominant Istvaeonic characteristics, some of which are also incorporated in German. Unlike German, Dutch (apart from Limburgish) has not been influenced at all by the south to north movement of the [High German consonant shift](/wiki/High_German_consonant_shift "High German consonant shift") and had some changes of its own.[[k]](#cite_note-61) The cumulation of these changes resulted over time in separate, but related standard languages with various degrees of similarities and differences between them. For a comparison between the West Germanic languages, see the sections Phonology, Grammar, and Vocabulary.

## Dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Dialects")]

Main article: [Dutch dialects and varieties](/wiki/Dutch_dialects_and_varieties "Dutch dialects and varieties")

Dutch [dialects](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect") are primarily the dialects that are both related with the Dutch language and are spoken in the same language area as the Dutch [standard language](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language"). Although heavily under the influence of the standard language, some of them remain diverse and are found in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") and in the [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") and [Flemish regions](/wiki/Flemish_region "Flemish region") of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"). The areas in which they are spoken often correspond with former medieval counties and duchies. The Netherlands (but not Belgium) distinguishes between a dialect and a *streektaal* ("[regional language](/wiki/Regional_language "Regional language")"). Those words are actually more political than linguistic because a regional language unites a large group of very different varieties. Such is the case with the [Gronings dialect](/wiki/Gronings_dialect "Gronings dialect"), which is considered a variety of the [Dutch Low Saxon](/wiki/Dutch_Low_Saxon "Dutch Low Saxon") regional language, but it is relatively distinct from other Dutch Low Saxon varieties. Also, some Dutch dialects are more remote from the Dutch standard language than some varieties of a regional language are. Within the Netherlands, a further distinction is made between a regional language and a separate language, which is the case with the ([standardised](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language")) [West Frisian language](/wiki/West_Frisian_language "West Frisian language"). It is spoken alongside Dutch in the province of [Friesland](/wiki/Friesland "Friesland").

Dutch dialects and regional languages are not spoken as often as they used to be, especially in the Netherlands. Recent research by Geert Driessen shows that the use of dialects and regional languages among both Dutch adults and youth is in heavy decline. In 1995, 27 percent of the Dutch adult population spoke a dialect or regional language on a regular basis, but in 2011, that was no more than 11 percent. In 1995, 12 percent of children of primary school age spoke a dialect or regional language, but in 2011, that had declined to four percent. Of the officially recognised regional languages [Limburgish](/wiki/Limburgish "Limburgish") is spoken the most (in 2011 among adults 54%, among children 31%) and Dutch Low Saxon the least (adults 15%, children 1%). The decline of the [West Frisian language](/wiki/West_Frisian_language "West Frisian language") in Friesland occupies a middle position (adults 44%, children 22%). Dialects are most often spoken in rural areas, but many cities have a distinct city dialect. For example, the city of [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent") has very distinct "g", "e" and "r" sounds that greatly differ from its surrounding villages. The [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") dialect combines Brabantian with words adopted from [Walloon](/wiki/Walloon_language "Walloon language") and [French](/wiki/French_language "French language").

Some dialects had, until recently, extensions across the borders of other standard language areas. In most cases, the heavy influence of the standard language has broken the [dialect continuum](/wiki/Dialect_continuum "Dialect continuum"). Examples are the [Gronings dialect](/wiki/Gronings_dialect "Gronings dialect") spoken in [Groningen](/wiki/Groningen_(province) "Groningen (province)") as well as the closely related varieties in adjacent [East Frisia](/wiki/East_Frisia "East Frisia") (Germany). [Kleverlandish](/wiki/Kleverlandish "Kleverlandish") is a dialect spoken in southern [Gelderland](/wiki/Gelderland "Gelderland"), the northern tip of [Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Netherlands) "Limburg (Netherlands)"), and northeast of [North Brabant](/wiki/North_Brabant "North Brabant") (Netherlands), but also in adjacent parts of [North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia "North Rhine-Westphalia") (Germany). [Limburgish](/wiki/Limburgish_language "Limburgish language") (*Limburgs*) is spoken in [Limburg (Belgium)](/wiki/Limburg_(Belgium) "Limburg (Belgium)") as well as in the remaining part of [Limburg (Netherlands)](/wiki/Limburg_(Netherlands) "Limburg (Netherlands)") and extends across the German border. West Flemish (*Westvlaams*) is spoken in [West Flanders](/wiki/West_Flanders "West Flanders"), the western part of [Zeelandic Flanders](/wiki/Zeelandic_Flanders "Zeelandic Flanders") and also in [French Flanders](/wiki/French_Flanders "French Flanders"), where it virtually became extinct to make way for French.

### Dialect groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Dialect groups")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/DUTCHDIALECTSMAP.PNG/250px-DUTCHDIALECTSMAP.PNG)](/wiki/File:DUTCHDIALECTSMAP.PNG)

Traditional division of Dutch dialects

The *[West Flemish](/wiki/West_Flemish "West Flemish")* group of dialects, spoken in [West Flanders](/wiki/West_Flanders "West Flanders") and [Zeeland](/wiki/Zeeland "Zeeland"), is so distinct that it might be considered as a separate language variant, although the strong significance of language in Belgian politics would prevent the government from classifying them as such. An oddity of the dialect is that, the [voiced velar fricative](/wiki/Voiced_velar_fricative "Voiced velar fricative") (written as "g" in Dutch) shifts to a [voiced glottal fricative](/wiki/Voiced_glottal_fricative "Voiced glottal fricative") (written as "h" in Dutch), while the letter "h" becomes mute (as in French). As a result, when West Flemings try to talk Standard Dutch, they are often unable to pronounce the g-sound, and pronounce it similar to the h-sound. This leaves, for example, no difference between "*held*" (hero) and "*geld*" (money). Or in some cases, they are aware of the problem, and hyper-correct the "h" into a voiced velar fricative or g-sound, again leaving no difference. The West Flemish variety historically spoken in adjacent parts in France is sometimes called [French Flemish](/wiki/French_Flemish "French Flemish") and is listed as a French [minority language](/wiki/Minority_language "Minority language"). However, only a very small and aging minority of the French-Flemish population still speaks and understands West Flemish.

*[Hollandic](/wiki/Hollandic_dialect "Hollandic dialect")* is spoken in [Holland](/wiki/Holland "Holland") and [Utrecht](/wiki/Utrecht "Utrecht"), though the original forms of this dialect (which were heavily influenced by a West Frisian [substratum](/wiki/Stratum_(linguistics)#Substratum "Stratum (linguistics)") and, from the 16th century on, by [Brabantian dialects](/wiki/Brabantian_dialect "Brabantian dialect")) are now relatively rare. The urban dialects of the [Randstad](/wiki/Randstad "Randstad"), which are Hollandic dialects, do not diverge from standard Dutch very much, but there is a clear difference between the city dialects of [Rotterdam](/wiki/Rotterdam "Rotterdam"), [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague"), [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam") and [Utrecht](/wiki/Utrecht "Utrecht"). In some rural Hollandic areas more authentic Hollandic dialects are still being used, especially north of Amsterdam. Another group of dialects based on Hollandic is that spoken in the cities and larger towns of [Friesland](/wiki/Friesland "Friesland"), where it partially displaced [West Frisian](/wiki/West_Frisian_language "West Frisian language") in the 16th century and is known as *[Stadsfries](/wiki/Stadsfries_dialects "Stadsfries dialects")* ("Urban Frisian"). Hollandic together with inter alia
[Kleverlandish](/wiki/Kleverlandish "Kleverlandish") and [North Brabantian](/w/index.php?title=North_Brabantian&action=edit&redlink=1 "North Brabantian (page does not exist)"), but without Stadsfries, are the [Central Dutch dialects](/wiki/Central_Dutch_dialects "Central Dutch dialects").

*[Brabantian](/wiki/Brabantian_language "Brabantian language")* is named after the historical [Duchy of Brabant](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant"), which corresponded mainly to the provinces of [North Brabant](/wiki/North_Brabant "North Brabant") and southern [Gelderland](/wiki/Gelderland "Gelderland"), the Belgian provinces of [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp_(province) "Antwerp (province)") and [Flemish Brabant](/wiki/Flemish_Brabant "Flemish Brabant"), as well as Brussels (where its native speakers have become a minority) and the province of [Walloon Brabant](/wiki/Walloon_Brabant "Walloon Brabant"). Brabantian expands into small parts in the west of [Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Belgium) "Limburg (Belgium)") while its strong influence on the [East Flemish](/wiki/East_Flemish "East Flemish") of [East Flanders](/wiki/East_Flanders "East Flanders") and eastern [Zeelandic Flanders](/wiki/Zeelandic_Flanders "Zeelandic Flanders")[[51]](#cite_note-62) weakens towards the west. In a small area in the northwest of North Brabant ([Willemstad](/wiki/Willemstad_(North_Brabant) "Willemstad (North Brabant)")), [Hollandic](/wiki/Hollandic "Hollandic") is spoken. Conventionally, the [Kleverlandish](/wiki/Kleverlandish "Kleverlandish") dialects are distinguished from Brabantian, but there are no objective criteria apart from geography to do so. Over 5 million people live in an area with some form of Brabantian being the predominant colloquial language out of the area's 22 million Dutch-speakers.[[52]](#cite_note-63)[[53]](#cite_note-64)

*[Limburgish](/wiki/Limburgish_language "Limburgish language")*, spoken in both [Belgian Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Belgium) "Limburg (Belgium)") and [Netherlands Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Netherlands) "Limburg (Netherlands)") and in adjacent parts in Germany, is considered a dialect in Belgium, while having obtained the official status of regional language in the Netherlands. Limburgish has been influenced by the [Ripuarian](/wiki/Ripuarian_language "Ripuarian language") varieties like the [Colognian dialect](/wiki/Colognian_dialect "Colognian dialect"), and has had a somewhat different development since the late Middle Ages.

### Regional languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Regional languages")]

Two dialect groups have been given the official status of [regional language](/wiki/Regional_language "Regional language") (or *streektaal*) in the Netherlands. Like several other dialect groups, both are part of a dialect continuum that continues across the national border.

#### Dutch Low Saxon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Dutch Low Saxon")]

Main article: [Dutch Low Saxon](/wiki/Dutch_Low_Saxon "Dutch Low Saxon")

The Dutch Low Saxon dialect area comprises the provinces of [Groningen](/wiki/Groningen_(province) "Groningen (province)"), [Drenthe](/wiki/Drenthe "Drenthe") and [Overijssel](/wiki/Overijssel "Overijssel"), as well as parts of the provinces of [Gelderland](/wiki/Gelderland "Gelderland"), [Flevoland](/wiki/Flevoland "Flevoland"), [Friesland](/wiki/Friesland "Friesland") and [Utrecht](/wiki/Utrecht "Utrecht"). This group, which is not Low Franconian but instead Low Saxon and close to neighbouring Low German, has been elevated by the Netherlands (and by Germany) to the legal status of *streektaal* ([regional language](/wiki/Regional_language "Regional language")) according to the [European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages](/wiki/European_Charter_for_Regional_or_Minority_Languages "European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages"). By some it is regarded as Dutch for a number of reasons. From the 14th to 15th century onward, its urban centers ([Deventer](/wiki/Deventer "Deventer"), [Zwolle](/wiki/Zwolle "Zwolle"), [Kampen](/wiki/Kampen,_Overijssel "Kampen, Overijssel"), [Zutphen](/wiki/Zutphen "Zutphen") and [Doesburg](/wiki/Doesburg "Doesburg")) have been increasingly influenced by the western written Dutch and became a linguistically mixed area. From the 17th century onward, it was gradually integrated into the Dutch language area.[[54]](#cite_note-65) Dutch Low Saxon used to be at one end of the Low German [dialect continuum](/wiki/Dialect_continuum "Dialect continuum"). However, the national border has given way to dialect boundaries coinciding with a political border, because the traditional dialects are strongly influenced by the national standard varieties.[[55]](#cite_note-66)

#### Limburgish

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Limburgish")]

Main article: [Limburgish](/wiki/Limburgish "Limburgish")

While a somewhat heterogeneous group of [Low Franconian](/wiki/Low_Franconian_languages "Low Franconian languages") dialects, Limburgish has received official status as a regional language in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), but not in Belgium. Due to this official recognition, it receives protection by chapter 2 of the [European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages](/wiki/European_Charter_for_Regional_or_Minority_Languages "European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages").[[56]](#cite_note-67)

### Daughter and sister languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Daughter and sister languages")]

[Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans"), although to a significant degree mutually intelligible with Dutch, is usually not considered a dialect but instead a separate [standardised language](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language"). It is spoken in South Africa and Namibia. As a daughter language of 17th-century Dutch dialects, Afrikaans evolved in parallel with modern Dutch, but was influenced by various [other languages](/wiki/Afrikaans#History "Afrikaans") in South Africa.

*[West Frisian](/wiki/West_Frisian_language "West Frisian language")* (*Westerlauwers Fries*), along with [Saterland Frisian](/wiki/Saterland_Frisian_language "Saterland Frisian language") and [North Frisian](/wiki/North_Frisian_language "North Frisian language"), evolved from the same branch of the [West Germanic languages](/wiki/West_Germanic_languages "West Germanic languages") as [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") (i.e. [Anglo-Frisian](/wiki/Anglo-Frisian_languages "Anglo-Frisian languages")) and are therefore genetically more closely related to English and Scots than to Dutch. The different influences on the respective languages, however, particularly that of Norman French on English and Dutch on West Frisian, have rendered English quite distinct from West Frisian, and West Frisian less distinct from Dutch than from English. Although under heavy influence of the Dutch standard language, it is not mutually intelligible with Dutch and considered a [sister language](/wiki/Sister_language "Sister language") of Dutch, like English and German.[[57]](#cite_note-68)

## Geographic distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Geographic distribution")]

See also: [Dutch diaspora](/wiki/Dutch_diaspora "Dutch diaspora") and [Geographical distribution of Dutch speakers](/wiki/Geographical_distribution_of_Dutch_speakers "Geographical distribution of Dutch speakers")

Approximate distribution of native Dutch speakers worldwide:

1. Netherlands (70.8%)
2. Belgium (27.1%)
3. Suriname (1.70%)
4. Caribbean (0.10%)
5. Other (0.30%)

Dutch First Language Speakers

| --- | --- | --- |

| Suriname | 400,000[[4]](#cite_note-Feiten-4) | 2020 |
