Dutch is an [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of the Netherlands proper (not enshrined in the [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Netherlands "Constitution of the Netherlands") but in administrative law[[62]](#cite_note-73)[[l]](#cite_note-notconstitution-77)), Belgium, Suriname, the Dutch Caribbean municipalities (St. Eustatius, Saba and Bonaire), [Aruba](/wiki/Aruba "Aruba"), [Curaçao](/wiki/Cura%C3%A7ao "Curaçao") and [Sint Maarten](/wiki/Sint_Maarten "Sint Maarten"). Dutch is also an official language of several international organisations, such as the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"),[[66]](#cite_note-78) [Union of South American Nations](/wiki/Union_of_South_American_Nations "Union of South American Nations")[[67]](#cite_note-79) and the [Caribbean Community](/wiki/Caribbean_Community "Caribbean Community"). At an academic level, Dutch is taught in about 175 universities in 40 countries. About 15,000 students worldwide study Dutch at university.[[68]](#cite_note-vraag-80)

### Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Europe")]

In Europe, Dutch is the majority language in the Netherlands (96%) and Belgium (59%) as well as a minority language in Germany and northern France's [French Flanders](/wiki/French_Flanders "French Flanders"). Though Belgium as a whole is multilingual, three of the four [language areas](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium") into which the country is divided ([Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders"), francophone [Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia"), and the [German-speaking Community](/wiki/German-speaking_Community_of_Belgium "German-speaking Community of Belgium")) are largely monolingual, with [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") being bilingual. The Netherlands and Belgium produce the vast majority of [music](/wiki/Music "Music"), [films](/wiki/Film "Film"), [books](/wiki/Book "Book") and other media written or spoken in Dutch.[[69]](#cite_note-trots-81) Dutch is a [monocentric language](/wiki/Pluricentric_language "Pluricentric language"), at least what concerns its written form, with all speakers using the same [standard form](/wiki/Standardization "Standardization") (authorised by the [Dutch Language Union](/wiki/Dutch_Language_Union "Dutch Language Union")) based on a [Dutch orthography](/wiki/Dutch_orthography "Dutch orthography") defined in the so-called "[Green Booklet](/wiki/Green_Booklet "Green Booklet")" authoritative dictionary and employing the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet") when writing; however, pronunciation varies between dialects. Indeed, in stark contrast to its written uniformity, Dutch lacks a unique [prestige dialect](/wiki/Prestige_(sociolinguistics) "Prestige (sociolinguistics)") and has a large dialectal continuum consisting of 28 main dialects, which can themselves be further divided into at least 600 distinguishable varieties.[[70]](#cite_note-82)[[71]](#cite_note-83) In the Netherlands, the [Hollandic dialect](/wiki/Hollandic_dialect "Hollandic dialect") dominates in national [broadcast media](/wiki/Broadcast_media "Broadcast media") while in Flanders [Brabantian dialect](/wiki/Brabantian_dialect "Brabantian dialect") dominates in that capacity, making them in turn unofficial [prestige dialects](/wiki/Prestige_dialect "Prestige dialect") in their respective countries.

Outside the Netherlands and Belgium, the dialect spoken in and around the German town of [Kleve](/wiki/Kleve "Kleve") ([Kleverlandish](/wiki/Kleverlandish "Kleverlandish")) is historically and genetically a [Low Franconian](/wiki/Low_Franconian "Low Franconian") variety. In North-Western France, the area around [Calais](/wiki/Calais "Calais") was historically Dutch-speaking (West Flemish), of which an estimated 20,000 are daily speakers. The cities of [Dunkirk](/wiki/Dunkirk "Dunkirk"), [Gravelines](/wiki/Gravelines "Gravelines") and [Bourbourg](/wiki/Bourbourg "Bourbourg") only became predominantly French-speaking by the end of the 19th century. In the countryside, until [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), many elementary schools continued to teach in Dutch, and the Catholic Church continued to preach and teach the [catechism](/wiki/Catechism "Catechism") in Dutch in many parishes.[[72]](#cite_note-84)

During the second half of the 19th century, Dutch was banned from all levels of education by both [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia") and France and lost most of its functions as a cultural language. In both Germany and France, the Dutch standard language is largely absent, and speakers of these Dutch dialects will use German or French in everyday speech. Dutch is not afforded legal status in France or Germany, either by the central or regional public authorities, and knowledge of the language is declining among younger generations.[[73]](#cite_note-85)

As a foreign language, Dutch is mainly taught in primary and secondary schools in areas adjacent to the Netherlands and [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders"). In [French-speaking Belgium](/wiki/French_Community_of_Belgium "French Community of Belgium"), over 300,000 pupils are enrolled in Dutch courses, followed by over 23,000 in the [German states](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") of [Lower Saxony](/wiki/Lower_Saxony "Lower Saxony") and [North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia "North Rhine-Westphalia"), and about 7,000 in the [French region](/wiki/Regions_of_France "Regions of France") of [Nord-Pas-de-Calais](/wiki/Nord-Pas-de-Calais "Nord-Pas-de-Calais") (of which 4,550 are in primary school).[[74]](#cite_note-wereldwijd-86) At an academic level, the largest number of faculties of *neerlandistiek* can be found in Germany (30 universities), followed by France (20 universities) and the United Kingdom (5 universities).[[74]](#cite_note-wereldwijd-86)[[75]](#cite_note-Neerlandistiek_wereldwijd-87)

### Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Asia")]

Further information: [Indonesian Dutch](/wiki/Indonesian_Dutch "Indonesian Dutch") and [List of Dutch loanwords in Indonesian](/wiki/List_of_Dutch_loanwords_in_Indonesian "List of Dutch loanwords in Indonesian")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Nederlandsindie.PNG/250px-Nederlandsindie.PNG)](/wiki/File:Nederlandsindie.PNG)

In the [Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies") (present day [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") and [Malacca](/wiki/Malacca "Malacca"), Malaysia), Dutch was used by only a limited educated elite.[[76]](#cite_note-88)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Kantor_Pos_Indonesia_cabang_Telanaipura_-_Telanaipura%2C_Kota_Jambi%2C_JA_%2823_April_2021%29.jpg/250px-Kantor_Pos_Indonesia_cabang_Telanaipura_-_Telanaipura%2C_Kota_Jambi%2C_JA_%2823_April_2021%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kantor_Pos_Indonesia_cabang_Telanaipura_-_Telanaipura,_Kota_Jambi,_JA_(23_April_2021).jpg)

[Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") did not adopt the Dutch language after independence. However, the [Indonesian language](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language") is heavily influenced by Dutch. Seen here is *kantor pos* (from the Dutch *postkantoor*), meaning *[post office](/wiki/Post_office "Post office")*.

Despite the Dutch presence in Indonesia for almost 350 years, as the Asian bulk of the [Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies"), the Dutch language has no official status there[[77]](#cite_note-89) and the small minority that can speak the language fluently are either educated members of the oldest generation, or employed in the legal profession such as historians, diplomats, lawyers, jurists and linguists/polyglots,[[78]](#cite_note-90) as certain [law codes](/wiki/Law_code "Law code") are still available only in Dutch.[[79]](#cite_note-91) Dutch is taught in various educational centres in Indonesia, the most important of which is the Erasmus Language Centre (ETC) in [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta"). Each year, some 1,500 to 2,000 students take Dutch courses there.[[80]](#cite_note-92) In total, several thousand Indonesians study Dutch as a foreign language.[[81]](#cite_note-93) Owing to centuries of Dutch rule in Indonesia, many old documents are written in Dutch. Many universities therefore include Dutch as a source language, mainly for law and history students.[[82]](#cite_note-94) In Indonesia this involves about 35,000 students.[[68]](#cite_note-vraag-80)

Unlike other European nations, the Dutch chose not to follow a policy of language expansion amongst the indigenous peoples of their colonies.[[83]](#cite_note-95) In the last quarter of the 19th century, however, a local elite gained [proficiency](/wiki/Language_proficiency "Language proficiency") in Dutch so as to meet the needs of expanding bureaucracy and business.[[84]](#cite_note-kol-96) Nevertheless, the Dutch government remained reluctant to teach Dutch on a large scale for fear of destabilising the colony. Dutch, the language of power, was supposed to remain in the hands of the leading elite.[[84]](#cite_note-kol-96)

After independence, Dutch was dropped as an official language and replaced by [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language"), but this does not mean that Dutch has completely disappeared in Indonesia: [Indonesian Dutch](/wiki/Indonesian_Dutch "Indonesian Dutch"), a regional variety of the Dutch, was still spoken by about 500,000 half-blood in Indonesia in 1985.[[85]](#cite_note-97) Yet the Indonesian language inherited many words from Dutch: words for everyday life as well as scientific and technological terms.[[86]](#cite_note-98) One scholar argues that 20% of Indonesian words can be traced back to Dutch words,[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaier2005-12) many of which are transliterated to reflect phonetic pronunciation e.g. **[kantoor](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/kantoor#Dutch "wikt:kantoor")** "office" in Indonesian is *kantor*, *handdoek* "towel" in Indonesian is *handuk*, or *bushalte* "bus stop" in Indonesian is *halte bus*. In addition, many Indonesian words are [calques](/wiki/Calque "Calque") of Dutch; for example, *rumah sakit* "hospital" is calqued on the Dutch *ziekenhuis* (literally "sickhouse"), *kebun binatang* "zoo" on *dierentuin* (literally "animal garden"), *undang-undang dasar* "constitution" from *grondwet* (literally "ground law"). These account for some of the [differences in vocabulary](/wiki/Differences_between_Malay_and_Indonesian#Vocabulary_differences "Differences between Malay and Indonesian") between Indonesian and Malay. Some [regional languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Indonesia "Languages of Indonesia") in Indonesia have some Dutch loanwords as well; for example, [Sundanese](/wiki/Sundanese_language "Sundanese language") word *Katel* or "frying pan" origin in Dutch is "*ketel*". The [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_language "Javanese language") word for "bike/[bicycle](/wiki/Bicycle "Bicycle")" "*pit*" can be traced back to its origin in Dutch "*fiets*". The [Malacca](/wiki/Malacca "Malacca") state of [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") was also colonized by the Dutch in its longest period that Malacca was under foreign control. In the 19th century, the [East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies") trade started to dwindle, and with it the importance of Malacca as a trading post. The Dutch state officially ceded Malacca to the British in 1825. It took until 1957 for Malaya to gain its independence.[[87]](#cite_note-99) Despite this, the Dutch language is rarely spoken in Malacca or Malaysia and only limited to foreign nationals able to speak the language.

### Oceania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Oceania")]

After the declaration of independence of Indonesia, [Western New Guinea](/wiki/Western_New_Guinea "Western New Guinea"), the "wild east" of the [Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies"), remained a Dutch colony until 1962, known as [Netherlands New Guinea](/wiki/Netherlands_New_Guinea "Netherlands New Guinea").[[88]](#cite_note-100) Despite prolonged Dutch presence, the Dutch language is not spoken by many Papuans, the colony having been ceded to Indonesia in 1963.

Dutch-speaking immigrant communities can also be found in Australia and New Zealand. The [2011 Australian census](/wiki/Census_in_Australia "Census in Australia") showed 37,248 people speaking Dutch at home.[[89]](#cite_note-101) At the [2006 New Zealand census](/wiki/2006_New_Zealand_census "2006 New Zealand census"), 26,982 people, or 0.70 percent of the total population, reported to speak Dutch to sufficient fluency that they could hold an everyday conversation.[[90]](#cite_note-102)

### Americas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Americas")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Suriname_in_its_region.svg/250px-Suriname_in_its_region.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Suriname_in_its_region.svg)

The location of [Suriname](/wiki/Suriname "Suriname") in [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/18/LocationNetherlandsAntillesWithAruba.png)](/wiki/File:LocationNetherlandsAntillesWithAruba.png)

The [Dutch Caribbean](/wiki/Dutch_Caribbean "Dutch Caribbean") at both ends of the [Lesser Antilles](/wiki/Lesser_Antilles "Lesser Antilles"), lining the [Caribbean Sea](/wiki/Caribbean_Sea "Caribbean Sea")

In contrast to the colonies in the [East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies"), from the second half of the 19th century onwards, the Netherlands envisaged the expansion of Dutch in its colonies in the [West Indies](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"). Until 1863, when [slavery](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery") was abolished in the West Indies, slaves were forbidden to speak Dutch, with the effect that local creoles such as [Papiamento](/wiki/Papiamento "Papiamento") and [Sranan Tongo](/wiki/Sranan_Tongo "Sranan Tongo") which were based not on Dutch but rather other European languages, became common in the Dutch West Indies. However, as most of the people in the [Colony of Surinam](/wiki/Surinam_(Dutch_colony) "Surinam (Dutch colony)") (now [Suriname](/wiki/Suriname "Suriname")) worked on Dutch plantations, this reinforced the use of Dutch as a means for direct communication.[[84]](#cite_note-kol-96)[[91]](#cite_note-103)

In [Suriname](/wiki/Suriname "Suriname") today, Dutch is the sole official language,[[92]](#cite_note-104) and over 60 percent of the population speaks it as a [mother tongue](/wiki/First_language "First language").[[93]](#cite_note-taalgebied-105) Dutch is the obligatory medium of instruction in schools in Suriname, even for non-native speakers.[[94]](#cite_note-106) A further twenty-four percent of the population speaks Dutch as a [second language](/wiki/Second_language "Second language").[[95]](#cite_note-107) Suriname gained its independence from the Netherlands in 1975 and has been an associate member of the [Dutch Language Union](/wiki/Dutch_Language_Union "Dutch Language Union") since 2004.[[96]](#cite_note-108) The [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") of Suriname, however, is [Sranan Tongo](/wiki/Sranan_Tongo "Sranan Tongo"),[[97]](#cite_note-109) spoken natively by about a fifth of the population.[[69]](#cite_note-trots-81)[[m]](#cite_note-110)

Dutch is official on all 6 [Dutch Caribbean](/wiki/Dutch_Caribbean "Dutch Caribbean") islands ([Aruba](/wiki/Aruba "Aruba"), [Bonaire](/wiki/Bonaire "Bonaire"), [Curaçao](/wiki/Cura%C3%A7ao "Curaçao"), [Sint Maarten](/wiki/Sint_Maarten "Sint Maarten"), [Saba](/wiki/Saba_(island) "Saba (island)") and [Sint Eustatius](/wiki/Sint_Eustatius "Sint Eustatius")), but is not commonly spoken on any of the islands. Dutch is spoken as a first language by only 7% to 8% of the population,[[98]](#cite_note-111) although most people on the Dutch Caribbean islands can speak Dutch to varying degrees of fluency as the education system is in Dutch at some or all levels.

Now-extinct [Dutch-based creole languages](/wiki/Dutch-based_creole_languages "Dutch-based creole languages") were formerly spoken in the [Virgin Islands](/wiki/Virgin_Islands "Virgin Islands") and [Guyana](/wiki/Guyana "Guyana") ([Negerhollands](/wiki/Negerhollands "Negerhollands"), [Berbice Dutch creole](/wiki/Berbice_Creole_Dutch "Berbice Creole Dutch"), [Skepi Dutch creole](/wiki/Skepi_Creole_Dutch "Skepi Creole Dutch")).

In the United States, a now extinct dialect of Dutch, [Jersey Dutch](/wiki/Jersey_Dutch "Jersey Dutch"), spoken by descendants of 17th-century Dutch settlers in Bergen and Passaic counties, was still spoken as late as 1921.[[99]](#cite_note-112) Other [Dutch-based creole languages](/wiki/Dutch-based_creole_languages "Dutch-based creole languages") once spoken in the Americas include [Mohawk Dutch](/wiki/Mohawk_Dutch "Mohawk Dutch") (in [Albany, New York](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York")), [Berbice](/wiki/Berbice_Creole_Dutch "Berbice Creole Dutch") (in Guyana), [Skepi](/wiki/Skepi_Creole_Dutch "Skepi Creole Dutch") (in [Essequibo, Guyana](/wiki/Essequibo_(colony) "Essequibo (colony)")) and [Negerhollands](/wiki/Negerhollands "Negerhollands") (in the [United States Virgin Islands](/wiki/United_States_Virgin_Islands "United States Virgin Islands")). [Pennsylvania Dutch](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Dutch "Pennsylvania Dutch") is not a member of the set of Dutch dialects and is less misleadingly called [Pennsylvania German](/wiki/Pennsylvania_German_language "Pennsylvania German language").[[100]](#cite_note-113)

[Martin Van Buren](/wiki/Martin_Van_Buren "Martin Van Buren"), the eighth [President of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), spoke Dutch natively[[101]](#cite_note-114) and is the only U.S. president whose first language was not English. Dutch prevailed for many generations as the dominant language in parts of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") along the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"). Another famous American born in this region who spoke Dutch as a first language was [Sojourner Truth](/wiki/Sojourner_Truth "Sojourner Truth").

According to the [2000 United States census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census"), 150,396 people spoke Dutch at home,[[102]](#cite_note-115) while according to the [2006 Canadian census](/wiki/Canada_2006_Census "Canada 2006 Census"), this number reaches 160,000 Dutch speakers.[[103]](#cite_note-116) At an academic level, 20 universities offer Dutch studies in the United States.[[74]](#cite_note-wereldwijd-86)[[75]](#cite_note-Neerlandistiek_wereldwijd-87) In Canada, Dutch is the fourth most spoken language by farmers, after English, French and German,[[104]](#cite_note-117) and the fifth most spoken non-official language overall (by 0.6% of Canadians).[[105]](#cite_note-118)

### Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Africa")]

Main article: [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans")

See also: [Differences between Afrikaans and Dutch](/wiki/Differences_between_Afrikaans_and_Dutch "Differences between Afrikaans and Dutch")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Stuttafords_Huisgenoot.jpg/250px-Stuttafords_Huisgenoot.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stuttafords_Huisgenoot.jpg)

Standard Dutch used in a 1916 ad in South Africa before Afrikaans replaced Dutch for use in media

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/South_Africa_2011_Afrikaans_speakers_proportion_map.svg/250px-South_Africa_2011_Afrikaans_speakers_proportion_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:South_Africa_2011_Afrikaans_speakers_proportion_map.svg)

The distribution of Afrikaans across South Africa: proportion of the population speaking Afrikaans at home:

* 0–20%
* 20–40%
* 40–60%
* 60–80%
* 80–100%

The largest legacy of the Dutch language lies in South Africa, which attracted large numbers of Dutch, Flemish and other northwest European farmer (in Dutch, *[boer](/wiki/Boer "Boer")*) settlers, all of whom were quickly assimilated.[[106]](#cite_note-Deumert-119) The long isolation from the rest of the Dutch-speaking world made the Dutch as spoken in Southern Africa evolve into what is now Afrikaans.[[107]](#cite_note-coetzee-120) In 1876, the first Afrikaans newspaper called *[Die Afrikaanse Patriot](/wiki/Die_Afrikaanse_Patriot "Die Afrikaanse Patriot")* was published in the Cape Colony.[[108]](#cite_note-121)

European Dutch remained the [literary language](/wiki/Literary_language "Literary language")[[107]](#cite_note-coetzee-120) until the start of the 1920s, when under pressure of [Afrikaner nationalism](/wiki/Afrikaner_nationalism "Afrikaner nationalism") the local "African" Dutch was preferred over the written, European-based standard.[[106]](#cite_note-Deumert-119) In 1925, section 137 of the 1909 constitution of the [Union of South Africa](/wiki/Union_of_South_Africa "Union of South Africa") was amended by Act 8 of 1925, stating "the word *Dutch* in article 137 ... is hereby declared to include Afrikaans".[[109]](#cite_note-122)[[110]](#cite_note-123) The constitution of 1983 only listed English and Afrikaans as official languages. It is estimated that between 90% and 95% of Afrikaans vocabulary is ultimately of Dutch origin.[[111]](#cite_note-124)[[112]](#cite_note-125)

Both languages are still largely mutually intelligible, although this relation can in some fields (such as lexicon, spelling and grammar) be asymmetric, as it is easier for Dutch speakers to understand written Afrikaans than it is for Afrikaans speakers to understand written Dutch.[[113]](#cite_note-126) Afrikaans is grammatically far less complex than Dutch, and vocabulary items are generally altered in a clearly patterned manner, e.g. *vogel* becomes *voël* ("bird") and *regen* becomes *reën* ("rain").[[114]](#cite_note-127) In South Africa, the number of students following Dutch at university is difficult to estimate, since the academic study of Afrikaans inevitably includes the study of Dutch.[[68]](#cite_note-vraag-80) Elsewhere in the world, the number of people learning Dutch is relatively small.

Afrikaans is the third largest language of South Africa in terms of native speakers (~13.5%),[[115]](#cite_note-Key_results_of_the_2011_census-128) of whom 53% are [Coloureds](/wiki/Coloureds "Coloureds") and 42.4% [Whites](/wiki/White_South_Africans "White South Africans").[[116]](#cite_note-129) In 1996, 40 percent of South Africans reported to know Afrikaans at least at a very basic level of communication.[[117]](#cite_note-130) It is the [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") in Namibia,[[106]](#cite_note-Deumert-119)[[118]](#cite_note-131)[[119]](#cite_note-132) where it is spoken natively in 11 percent of households.[[120]](#cite_note-133) In total, Afrikaans is the [first language](/wiki/First_language "First language") in South Africa alone of about 7.1 million people[[115]](#cite_note-Key_results_of_the_2011_census-128) and is estimated to be a [second language](/wiki/Second_language "Second language") for at least 10 million people worldwide,[[121]](#cite_note-134) compared to over 23 million[[93]](#cite_note-taalgebied-105) and 5 million respectively, for Dutch.[[2]](#cite_note-Eurobarometer_Languages-2)

The Dutch colonial presence elsewhere in Africa, notably the [Dutch Gold Coast](/wiki/Dutch_Gold_Coast "Dutch Gold Coast"), was too ephemeral not to be wiped out by prevailing colonising European successors. Likewise, the Belgian colonial presence in the [Congo](/wiki/Belgian_Congo "Belgian Congo") and [Ruanda-Urundi](/wiki/Ruanda-Urundi "Ruanda-Urundi") ([Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda") and [Burundi](/wiki/Burundi "Burundi") held under a [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") mandate and later a UN trust territory) left little Dutch (Flemish) legacy, as French was the main colonial language.[[122]](#cite_note-135)

## Phonology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Phonology")]

Main article: [Dutch phonology](/wiki/Dutch_phonology "Dutch phonology")

Spoken Dutch, with a Netherlands (Brabantian) accent



Spoken Standard Dutch, with a [West Flemish](/wiki/West_Flemish "West Flemish") accent

For further details on different realisations of phonemes, dialectal differences and example words, see the full article at [Dutch phonology](/wiki/Dutch_phonology "Dutch phonology").

### Consonants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Consonants")]

Unlike other Germanic languages, Dutch has no phonological [aspiration of consonants](/wiki/Aspirated_consonants "Aspirated consonants").[[123]](#cite_note-136) Like most other Germanic languages, the Dutch [consonant](/wiki/Consonant "Consonant") system did not undergo the [High German consonant shift](/wiki/High_German_consonant_shift "High German consonant shift") and has a [syllable](/wiki/Syllable "Syllable") structure that allows fairly-complex [consonant clusters](/wiki/Consonant_cluster "Consonant cluster"). Dutch also retains full use of the [velar](/wiki/Velar_consonant "Velar consonant") [fricatives](/wiki/Fricative "Fricative") of [Proto-Germanic](/wiki/Proto-Germanic_language "Proto-Germanic language") that were lost or modified in many other Germanic languages. Dutch has [final-obstruent devoicing](/wiki/Final-obstruent_devoicing "Final-obstruent devoicing"). At the end of a word, voicing distinction is neutralised and all obstruents are pronounced voiceless. For example, Dutch **goede** (̇'good') is [ˈɣudə] but the related form **goed** is [ɣut]. Dutch shares this [final-obstruent devoicing](/wiki/Final-obstruent_devoicing "Final-obstruent devoicing") with German (the Dutch noun *goud* is pronounced [ɣɑut], the adjective *gouden* is pronounced [ɣɑudə(n)], like the German noun *Gold*, pronounced [ɡɔlt], adjective *golden*, pronounced [ɡɔldn] vs English *gold* and *golden*, both pronounced with [d].)

Voicing of pre-vocalic initial [voiceless alveolar fricatives](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolar_fricative "Voiceless alveolar fricative") occurs in standard Dutch like in German (Dutch *zeven*, German *sieben* with [z] versus English *seven*, Low Saxon *seven* with [s]), and also the shift /θ/ → /d/. Dutch shares only with Low German the development of /xs/ → /ss/ (Dutch *vossen*, *ossen* and Low German *Vösse*, *Ossen* versus German *Füchse*, *Ochsen* and English *foxes*, *oxen*), and also the development of /ft/ → /xt/ though it is far more common in Dutch (Dutch *zacht* and Low German *sacht* versus German *sanft* and English *soft*, but Dutch *kracht* versus Low German/German *Kraft* and English *craft*).

Notes:

* [ʔ] is not a separate phoneme in Dutch, but is inserted before vowel-initial syllables within words after /a/ and /ə/ and often also at the beginning of a word.
* The realisation of /r/ phoneme varies considerably from dialect to dialect and even between speakers in the same dialect area. Common realisations are an [alveolar trill](/wiki/Alveolar_trill "Alveolar trill") [r], [alveolar tap](/wiki/Alveolar_tap "Alveolar tap") [ɾ], [uvular trill](/wiki/Uvular_trill "Uvular trill") [ʀ], [voiced uvular fricative](/wiki/Voiced_uvular_fricative "Voiced uvular fricative") [ʁ], and [alveolar approximant](/wiki/Alveolar_approximant "Alveolar approximant") [ɹ].
* The realisation of /ʋ/ also varies somewhat by area and speaker. The main realisation is a [labiodental approximant](/wiki/Labiodental_approximant "Labiodental approximant") [ʋ], but some speakers, particularly in the south, use a [bilabial approximant](/wiki/Voiced_bilabial_fricative "Voiced bilabial fricative") [β̞] or a [labiovelar approximant](/wiki/Voiced_labio-velar_approximant "Voiced labio-velar approximant") [w].
* The lateral /l/ is slightly velarised postvocalically in most dialects, particularly in the north.[[124]](#cite_note-verhoeven2005-137)
* /x/ and /ɣ/ may be true velars [x] and [ɣ], uvular [χ] and [ʁ] or palatal [ç] and [ʝ]. The more palatal realisations are common in southern areas, and uvulars are common in the north.
* Some northern dialects have a tendency to devoice all fricatives, regardless of environment, which is particularly common with /ɣ/ but can affect others as well.
* /ɕ/, /ʑ/, /tɕ/, and /dʑ/ are not native phonemes of Dutch and usually occur in borrowed words, like *show* and *bagage* ('baggage'), but may occur if /s/, /z/, /t/, and /d/ are palatalised.
* /ɡ/ is not a native phoneme of Dutch and occurs only in borrowed words, like *baguette*.

### Vowels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Vowels")]

Like English, Dutch did not develop [i-mutation](/wiki/I-mutation "I-mutation") as a morphological marker and shares with most other Germanic languages the lengthening of short [vowels](/wiki/Vowels "Vowels") in [stressed](/wiki/Stress_(linguistics) "Stress (linguistics)") open [syllables](/wiki/Syllables "Syllables"), which has led to contrastive [vowel length](/wiki/Vowel_length "Vowel length") being used as a [morphological](/wiki/Morphology_(linguistics) "Morphology (linguistics)") marker. Dutch has an extensive [vowel](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel") inventory. Vowels can be grouped as back rounded, front unrounded and front rounded. They are also traditionally distinguished by length or [tenseness](/wiki/Tenseness "Tenseness").

Vowel length is not always considered a distinctive feature in Dutch phonology because it normally occurs with changes in vowel [quality](/wiki/Formant "Formant"). One feature or the other may be considered redundant, and some phonemic analyses prefer to treat it as an opposition of [tenseness](/wiki/Tenseness "Tenseness"). However, even if it is not considered part of the phonemic opposition, the long/tense vowels are still realised as *phonetically* longer than their short counterparts. The changes in vowel quality are also not always the same in all dialects, some of which may be little difference at all, with length remaining the primary distinguishing feature. Although all older words pair vowel length with a change in vowel quality, new loanwords have reintroduced phonemic oppositions of length. Compare *zonne(n)* [ˈzɔnə] ("suns") versus *zone* [ˈzɔːnə] ("zone") versus *zonen* [ˈzoːnə(n)] ("sons"), or *kroes* [krus] ("mug") versus *cruise* [kruːs] ("cruise").

* The long close-mid vowels /eː øː oː/ are realised as slightly closing diphthongs [ei øy ou] in many northern dialects.
* The long open-mid vowels /ɛː œː ɔː/ occur only in a handful of loanwords, mostly from French. In certain Belgian Dutch varieties, they may also occur as realisations of /ɛi œy ɔu/.[[124]](#cite_note-verhoeven2005-137)
* The long close and close-mid vowels are often pronounced more closed or as centering diphthongs before an /r/ in the syllable coda, which may occur before coda /l/ as well.

### Diphthongs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Diphthongs")]

See also: [IJ (digraph)](/wiki/IJ_(digraph) "IJ (digraph)")

Unique to the development of Dutch is the collapse of older *ol*/*ul*/*al* + [dental](/wiki/Dental_consonant "Dental consonant") into *ol* + dental, followed by vocalisation of pre-[consonantal](/wiki/Consonant "Consonant") /l/ and after a short vowel. That created the diphthong /ɑu/: Dutch *goud*, *zout* and *bout* corresponds with Low German *Gold*, *Solt*, *Bolt*; German *Gold*, *Salz*, *Balt* and English *gold*, *salt*, *bolt*. It is the most common diphthong, along with /ɛi œy/. All three are the only ones commonly considered unique phonemes in Dutch. The tendency for native English speakers is to pronounce Dutch names with /ɛi/ (written as *ij* or *ei*) as /aɪ/, (like the English "long i") does not normally lead to confusion for native listeners since in a number of dialects (such as in Amsterdam[[125]](#cite_note-138)), the same pronunciation is heard.

In contrast, /ɑi/ and /ɔi/ are rare in Dutch. The "long/tense" diphthongs are indeed realised as proper diphthongs but are generally analysed phonemically as a long/tense vowel, followed by a glide /j/ or /ʋ/. All diphthongs end in a close vowel (/i y u/) and are grouped here by their first element.

### Phonotactics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Phonotactics")]

The [syllable structure](/wiki/Syllable_structure "Syllable structure") of Dutch is (C)(C)(C)V(C)(C)(C)(C). Many words, as in English, begin with three consonants: *straat* /straːt/ (street). There are words that end in four consonants: *herfst* /ɦɛrfst/ (autumn), *ergst* /ɛrxst/ (worst), *interessantst* /ɪn.tə.rɛ.sɑntst/ (most interesting), *sterkst* /stɛrkst/ (strongest), the last three of which are [superlative](/wiki/Superlative "Superlative") adjectives.

The highest number of consonants in a single cluster is found in the word *slechtstschrijvend* /ˈslɛxtstˌsxrɛi̯vənt/ (writing worst), with seven consonant phonemes. *angstschreeuw* [/ˈɑŋstsxreːu̯/](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/1/1e/Angstschreeuw.ogg/Angstschreeuw.ogg.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Angstschreeuw.ogg "File:Angstschreeuw.ogg") (scream in fear) has six in a row.

### Polder Dutch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Polder Dutch")]

A notable change in pronunciation has been occurring in younger generations in the Dutch provinces of [Utrecht](/wiki/Utrecht_(province) "Utrecht (province)"), [North](/wiki/North_Holland "North Holland") and [South Holland](/wiki/South_Holland "South Holland"), which has been dubbed "Polder Dutch" by Jan Stroop.[[126]](#cite_note-139) Such speakers pronounce ⟨ij/ei⟩, ⟨ou/au⟩ and ⟨ui⟩, which used to be pronounced respectively as /ɛi/, /ɔu/, and /œy/, as increasingly lowered [ai], [au], and [ay]; respectively. In addition, the same speakers pronounce /eː/, /oː/, and /øː/ as the [diphthongs](/wiki/Diphthongs "Diphthongs") [ɛi], [ɔu], and [œy][[127]](#cite_note-140) respectively, making the change an example of a [chain shift](/wiki/Chain_shift "Chain shift").

The change is interesting from a sociolinguistic point of view because it has apparently happened relatively recently, in the 1970s and was pioneered by older well-educated women from the upper middle classes.[[128]](#cite_note-141) The lowering of the diphthongs has long been current in many Dutch dialects and is comparable to the English [Great Vowel Shift](/wiki/Great_Vowel_Shift "Great Vowel Shift") and the diphthongisation of long high vowels in Modern [High German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), which had centuries earlier reached the state now found in Polder Dutch. Stroop theorises that the lowering of open-mid to open diphthongs is a phonetically "natural" and inevitable development and that Dutch, after it had diphthongised the long high vowels like German and English, "should" have lowered the diphthongs like German and English as well.

Instead, he argues that the development has been artificially frozen in an "intermediate" state by the standardisation of Dutch pronunciation in the 16th century in which lowered diphthongs found in rural dialects were perceived as ugly by the educated classes and were accordingly declared substandard. Now, however, he thinks that the newly-affluent and independent women can afford to let that natural development take place in their speech. Stroop compares the role of Polder Dutch with the urban variety of British English pronunciation called [Estuary English](/wiki/Estuary_English "Estuary English").

This change is not taking place in [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans") (which instead has diphthongized the mid monophthongs /eː, øː, oː/ to [iə, yə, uə]), nor for Dutch speakers outside [Utrecht](/wiki/Utrecht_(province) "Utrecht (province)") and [Holland](/wiki/Holland "Holland").

## Grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Grammar")]

Main article: [Dutch grammar](/wiki/Dutch_grammar "Dutch grammar")

See also: [DT-Manie](/wiki/DT-Manie "DT-Manie")

Dutch is grammatically similar to [German](/wiki/German_grammar "German grammar"), such as in [syntax](/wiki/Syntax "Syntax") and verb [morphology](/wiki/Morphology_(linguistics) "Morphology (linguistics)") (for verb morphology in English verbs, Dutch and German, see [Germanic weak verb](/wiki/Germanic_weak_verb "Germanic weak verb") and [Germanic strong verb](/wiki/Germanic_strong_verb "Germanic strong verb")). [Grammatical cases](/wiki/Grammatical_case "Grammatical case") have largely become limited to pronouns and many [set phrases](/wiki/Set_phrase "Set phrase"). Inflected forms of the articles are often grace surnames and toponyms.

Standard Dutch uses three [genders](/wiki/Gender_in_Dutch_grammar "Gender in Dutch grammar") across natural and grammatical genders but for most non-Belgian speakers, masculine and feminine have merged to form the common gender (with *de* for "the"). The neuter (which uses *het*) remains distinct. This is similar to those of most [Continental Scandinavian tongues](/wiki/North_Germanic_languages#Classification "North Germanic languages"). Less so than English, inflectional grammar (such as in adjectival and noun endings) has simplified.

### Verbs and tenses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Verbs and tenses")]

When grouped according to their conjugational class, Dutch has four main verb types: [weak verbs](/wiki/Germanic_weak_verb "Germanic weak verb"), [strong verbs](/wiki/Germanic_strong_verb "Germanic strong verb"), [irregular verbs](/wiki/Irregular_verb "Irregular verb") and mixed verbs.

Weak verbs are most numerous, constituting about 60% of all verbs. In these, the past tense and past participle are formed with a dental suffix:

* Weak verbs with past in *-de*
* Weak verbs with past in *-te*

Strong verbs are the second most numerous verb group. This group is characterised by a vowel alternation of the stem in the past tense and perfect participle. Dutch distinguishes between 7 classes, comprising almost all strong verbs, with some internal variants. Dutch has many 'half strong verbs': these have a weak past tense and a strong participle or a strong past tense and a weak participle. The following table shows the vowel alternations in more detail. It also shows the number of roots (bare verbs) that belong to each class, variants with a prefix are excluded.

| Verb class | Verb | | Present | | Past | | Participle | | Number of roots |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **1** | *kijken* | (to watch) | ɛi | *k**ij**k* | eː | *k**ee**k* | eː | *gek**e**ken* | 58 |
| **2a** | *bieden* | (to offer) | i | *b**ie**d* | oː | *b**oo**d* | oː | *geb**o**den* | 17 |
| **2b** | *stuiven* | (to gush) | œy | *st**ui**f* | oː | *st**oo**f* | oː | *gest**o**ven* | 23 |
| **3a** | *klimmen* | (to climb) | ɪ | *kl**i**m* | ɔ | *kl**o**m* | ɔ | *gekl**o**mmen* | 25 |
| **3b** | *zenden* | (to send) | ɛ | *z**e**nd* | ɔ | *z**o**nd* | ɔ | *gez**o**nden* | 18 |
| **3 + 7** | *sterven* | (to die) | ɛ | *st**e**rf* | i | *st**ie**rf* | ɔ | *gest**o**rven* | 6 |
| **4** | *breken* | (to break) | eː | *br**ee**k* | ɑ ~ aː | *br**a**k ~ br**a**ken* | oː | *gebr**o**ken* | 7 |
| **4** irregular | *wegen* | (to weigh) | eː | *w**ee**g* | oː | *w**oo**g* | oː | *gew**o**gen* | 3 |
| **5** | *geven* | (to give) | eː | *g**ee**f* | ɑ ~ aː | *g**a**f ~ g**a**ven* | eː | *geg**e**ven* | 10 |
| **5** irregular | *zitten* | (to sit) | ɪ | *z**i**t* | ɑ ~ aː | *z**a**t ~ z**a**ten* | eː | *gez**e**ten* | 3 |
| **6** | *dragen* | (to carry) | aː | *dr**aa**g* | u | *dr**oe**g* | aː | *gedr**a**gen* | 4 |
| **7** | *roepen* | (to call) | X | *r**oe**p* | i | *r**ie**p* | X | *ger**oe**pen* | 8 |
| **7** irregular | *vangen* | (to catch) | X | *v**a**ng* | ɪ | *v**i**ng* | X | *gev**a**ngen* | 3 |
| Half strong past | *vragen* | (to ask) |  | *vraag* |  | *vroeg* |  | *gevraag**d*** | 3 |
| Half strong perfect | *bakken* | (to bake) |  | *bak* |  | *bak**te*** |  | *gebakken* | 19 |
| Other | *scheppen* | (to create) |  | *sch**e**p* |  | *sch**ie**p* |  | *gesch**a**pen* | 5 |

There is an ongoing process of "weakening" of strong verbs. The verb "*ervaren*" (to experience) used to be strictly a class 6 strong verb, having the past tense "*ervoer*" and participle "*ervaren*", but the weak form "*ervaarde*" for both past tense and participle is currently also in use. Some other verbs that were originally strong such as "*raden*" (to guess) and "*stoten*" (to bump), have past tense forms "*ried*" and "*stiet*" that are at present far less common than their weakened forms; "*raadde*" and "*stootte*".[[129]](#cite_note-142) In most examples of such weakened verbs that were originally strong, both their strong and weak formations are deemed correct.

### Genders and cases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Genders and cases")]

As in English, the case system of Dutch and the [subjunctive](/wiki/Subjunctive "Subjunctive") have largely fallen out of use, and the system has generalised the [dative](/wiki/Dative "Dative") over the [accusative](/wiki/Accusative "Accusative") [case](/wiki/Case_(linguistics) "Case (linguistics)") for certain [pronouns](/wiki/Pronoun "Pronoun") (NL: *me*, *je*; EN: *me*, *you*; LI: *mi*, *di* vs. DE: *mich/mir*, *dich/dir*). While standard Dutch has three [grammatical genders](/wiki/Gender_in_Dutch_grammar "Gender in Dutch grammar"), this has few consequences and the masculine and feminine gender are usually merged into a common gender in the Netherlands but not in Belgium (EN: none; NL/LI: common and neuter; in Belgium masculine, feminine and neuter is in use).

Modern Dutch has mostly lost its case system.[[130]](#cite_note-143) However, certain idioms and expressions continue to include now archaic case declensions. The definite article has just two forms, *de* and *het*, more complex than English, which has only *the*. The use of the older inflected form *den* in the dative and accusative, as well as use of *der* in the dative, is restricted to numerous set phrases, surnames and toponyms. But some dialects still use both, particularly "*der*" is often used instead of "*haar*" (her).

|  | Masculine singular | Feminine singular | Neuter singular | Plural (any gender) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Nominative | *de* | *de* | *het* | *de* |
| Genitive | *van de* | *van de* | *van het* | *van de* |
| Genitive | *des* | *der* | *des* | *der* |

In modern Dutch, the genitive articles *des* and *der* in the bottom line are commonly used in [idioms](/wiki/Idioms "Idioms"). Other usage is typically considered archaic, poetic or stylistic. One must know whether a noun is masculine or feminine to use them correctly. In most circumstances, the preposition *van*, the middle line, is instead used, followed by the normal article *de* or *het*, and in that case it makes no difference whether a word is masculine or feminine. For the idiomatic use of the articles in the genitive, see for example:

* Masculine singular: "***des** duivel**s***" (*lit*: "of the devil") (common proverbial meaning: Seething with rage)
* Feminine singular: "*het woordenboek **der** Friese taal*" ("the dictionary of the Frisian language")
* Neuter singular: "*de vrouw **des** huiz**es***" ("the lady of the house")
* Plural: de voortgang "***der** werken*" ("the progress of (public) works")

In contemporary usage, the [genitive case](/wiki/Genitive_case "Genitive case") still occurs a little more often with plurals than with singulars, as the plural article is *der* for all genders and no special noun inflection must be taken account of. *Der* is commonly used in order to avoid reduplication of *van*, e.g. *het merendeel **der** gedichten **van de** auteur* instead of *het merendeel **van de** gedichten **van de** auteur* ("the bulk of the author's poems").

There is also a genitive form for the pronoun *die/dat* ("that [one], those [ones]"), namely *diens* for masculine and neuter singulars (occurrences of *dier* for feminine singular and all plurals are extremely rare). Although usually avoided in common speech, this form can be used instead of possessive pronouns to avoid confusion. Compare:

* *Hij vertelde over zijn zoon en **zijn** vrouw.* – He spoke about his son and **his (own)** wife.
* *Hij vertelde over zijn zoon en **diens** vrouw.* – He spoke about his son and **the latter's** wife.

Analogically, the relative and interrogative pronoun *wie* ("who") has the genitive forms *wiens* and *wier* (corresponding to English *whose*, but less frequent in use).

Dutch also has a range of fixed expressions that make use of the genitive articles, which can be abbreviated using apostrophes. Common examples include "*'s ochtends*" (with *'s* as abbreviation of *des*; "in the morning") and *desnoods* (lit: "of the need", translated: "if necessary").

The Dutch written grammar has simplified over the past 100 years: [cases](/wiki/Grammatical_case "Grammatical case") are now mainly used for the pronouns, such as *ik* (I), *mij, me* (me), *mijn* (my), *wie* (who), *wiens* (whose: masculine or neuter singular), *wier* (whose: feminine singular; masculine, feminine or neuter plural). Nouns and adjectives are not case inflected (except for the genitive of proper nouns (names): -s, -'s or -'). In the spoken language cases and case inflections had already gradually disappeared from a much earlier date on (probably the 15th century) as in many continental West Germanic dialects.

Inflection of adjectives is more complicated. The adjective receives no ending with indefinite neuter nouns in singular (as with *een* /ən/ 'a/an'), and *-e* in all other cases. (This was also the case in Middle English, as in "a good**e** man".) *Fiets* belongs to the masculine/feminine category, while *water* and *huis* are neuter.

|  | Masculine singular or feminine singular | Neuter singular | Plural (any gender) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Definite (with definite article or pronoun) | *de mooi**e** fiets* ("the beautiful bicycle") *onze mooi**e** fiets* ("our beautiful bicycle") *deze mooi**e** fiets* ("this beautiful bicycle") | *het mooi**e** huis* ("the beautiful house") *ons mooi**e** huis* ("our beautiful house") *dit mooi**e** huis* ("this beautiful house") | *de mooi**e** fietsen* ("the beautiful bicycles") *de mooi**e** huizen* ("the beautiful houses") *onze mooi**e** fietsen* ("our beautiful bicycles") *deze mooi**e** huizen* ("these beautiful houses") |
| Indefinite (with indefinite article or no article and no pronoun) | *een mooi**e** fiets* ("a beautiful bicycle") *koud**e** soep* ("cold soup") | *een mooi huis* ("a beautiful house") *koud water* ("cold water") | *mooi**e** fietsen* ("beautiful bicycles") *mooi**e** huizen* ("beautiful houses") |

An adjective has no **e** if it is in the [predicative](/wiki/Predicative_(adjectival_or_nominal) "Predicative (adjectival or nominal)"): *De soep is koud.*

More complex inflection is still found in certain lexicalised expressions like *de heer de**s** hui**zes*** (literally, "the man of the house"), etc. These are usually remnants of cases (in this instance, the genitive case which is still used in German, cf. *Der Herr des Hauses*) and other inflections no longer in general use today. In such lexicalised expressions remnants of strong and weak nouns can be found too, e.g. *in het jaar de**s** Her**en*** (Anno Domini), where *-en* is actually the genitive ending of the weak noun. Similarly in some place names: *[**‌'s**-Grav**en**brakel](/wiki/Braine-le-Comte "Braine-le-Comte"), [**‌'s**-Hertog**en**bosch](/wiki/%27s-Hertogenbosch "'s-Hertogenbosch")*, etc. (with weak genitives of *graaf* "count", *hertog* "duke"). Also in this case, German [retains this feature](/wiki/German_grammar#Irregular_declensions "German grammar").

### Word order

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Word order")]

Dutch shares much of its word order with German. Dutch exhibits [subject–object–verb](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93object%E2%80%93verb "Subject–object–verb") word order, but in main clauses the [conjugated verb](/wiki/Dutch_conjugation "Dutch conjugation") is moved into the second position in what is known as verb second or [V2 word order](/wiki/V2_word_order "V2 word order"). This makes Dutch word order almost identical to that of German, but often different from English, which has [subject–verb–object](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93verb%E2%80%93object "Subject–verb–object") word order and has since lost the V2 word order that existed in [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English").[[131]](#cite_note-144)

An example sentence used in some Dutch language courses and textbooks is "*Ik kan mijn pen niet vinden omdat het veel te donker is*", which translates into English word for word as "*I can my pen not find because it far too dark is*", but in standard English word order would be written "*I cannot find my pen because it is far too dark*". If the sentence is split into a main and subclause and the verbs highlighted, the logic behind the word order can be seen.

Main clause: "*Ik **kan** mijn pen niet **vinden***"

Verb infinitives are placed in final position, but the finite, conjugated verb, in this case "*kan*" (can), is made the second element of the clause.

In subordinate clauses: "*omdat het veel te donker **is***", the verb or verbs always go in the final position.

In an [interrogative](/wiki/Interrogative "Interrogative") main clause the usual word order is: conjugated verb followed by subject; other verbs in final position:

* "*Kun jij je pen niet vinden?*" (literally "*Can you your pen not find?*") "*Can't you find your pen?*"

In the Dutch equivalent of a [wh-question](/wiki/Wh-question "Wh-question") the word order is: interrogative pronoun (or expression) + conjugated verb + subject; other verbs in final position:

* "*Waarom kun jij je pen niet vinden?*" ("*Why can you your pen not find?*") "*Why can't you find your pen?*"

In a [tag question](/wiki/Tag_question "Tag question") the word order is the same as in a declarative clause:

* "*Jij kunt je pen niet vinden?*" ("*You can your pen not find?*") "*You can't find your pen?*"

A subordinate clause does not change its word order:

* "*Kun jij je pen niet vinden omdat het veel te donker is?*" ("*Can you your pen not find because it far too dark is?*") "*Can you not find your pen because it's far too dark?*"

### Diminutives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Diminutives")]

See also: [List of diminutives by language § Dutch](/wiki/List_of_diminutives_by_language#Dutch "List of diminutives by language")

In Dutch, the diminutive is used extensively. The nuances of meaning expressed by the diminutive are a distinctive aspect of Dutch, and can be difficult for non-native speakers to master. It is very productive[[132]](#cite_note-Donaldson-145): 61 and formed by adding one of the [suffixes](/wiki/Affix "Affix") to the noun in question, depending on the latter's phonological ending:

* *-je* for ending in -b, -c, -d, -t, -f, -g, -ch, -k, -p, -v, -x, -z or -s: neef → neef*je* (*male cousin*, *nephew*)
* *-pje* for ending in -m: boom (*tree*) → boom*pje*
* *-kje* for ending in -ing if the preceding syllable carries the stress: koning (*king*) → konin*kje* (the 'ng'-sound transforms into 'nk'); but ring → ring*etje* (*ring*), and vondeling → vondeling*etje* (*foundling*) without this stress pattern
* *-tje* for ending in -h, -j, -l, -n, -r, -w, or a vowel other than -y: zoen → zoen*tje* (*kiss*). A single open vowel is doubled when adding "-tje" would change the pronunciation: aut*o* → aut*oo*tje (*car*).
* *-′tje* for ending in -y and for abbreviations: baby → baby'tje, cd → cd'tje, [A4](/wiki/A4_paper "A4 paper") → A4'tje
* *-etje* for ending in -b, -l, -n, -ng or -r preceded by a "short" (lax) vowel: bal → ball*etje* (*ball*). Final consonant is doubled (except for -ng) to preserve the vowel's shortness.

The diminutive suffixes *-ke* (from which *-tje* has derived by [palatalisation](/wiki/Palatalization_(sound_change) "Palatalization (sound change)")), *-eke*, *-ske*, *-ie* (only for words ending -ch, -k, -p, or -s), *-kie* (instead of -kje), and *-pie* (instead of -pje) are used in southern dialects, and the forms ending on -ie as well in northern urban dialects. Some of these form part of expressions that became standard language, like *een makkie*, from ge*mak* = *ease*). The noun joch (*young boy*) has, exceptionally, only the diminutive form jochie, also in standard Dutch. The form -ke is also found in many women's given names: Janneke, Marieke, Marijke, Mieke, Meike etc.

In Dutch, the diminutive is not restricted to nouns, but can be applied to [numerals](/wiki/Numeral_(linguistics) "Numeral (linguistics)") (*met z'n tweetjes*, "the two of us"), [pronouns](/wiki/Pronoun "Pronoun") (*onderonsje*, "tête-à-tête"), [verbal particles](/wiki/Verb "Verb") (*moetje*, "shotgun marriage"), and even [prepositions](/wiki/Preposition "Preposition") (*toetje*, "dessert").[[132]](#cite_note-Donaldson-145): 64–65 [Adjectives](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective") and [adverbs](/wiki/Adverbs "Adverbs") commonly take diminutive forms; the former take a diminutive ending and thus function as nouns, while the latter remain adverbs and always have the diminutive with the *-s* appended, e.g. adjective: groen ("green") → noun: groen*tje* ("rookie"); adverb: even ("a while") → adverb: even*tjes* ("a little while").

Some nouns have two different diminutives, each with a different meaning: bloem (*flower*) → bloem*pje* (lit. 'small flower'), but bloem*etje* (lit. also "small flower", meaning *[bouquet](/wiki/Flower_bouquet "Flower bouquet")*). A few nouns exist solely in a diminutive form, e.g. zeepaardje (*[seahorse](/wiki/Seahorse "Seahorse")*), while many, e.g. meisje (*girl*), originally a diminutive of meid (*maid*), have acquired a meaning [independent of their non-diminutive forms](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/wikt:Categorie:Zelfstandig_verkleinwoord "nl:wikt:Categorie:Zelfstandig verkleinwoord"). A diminutive can sometimes be added to an uncountable noun to refer to a single portion: ijs (*ice*, *ice cream*) → ijsje (*ice cream treat*, *cone of ice cream*), bier (*beer*) → biertje. Some diminutive forms only exist in the plural, e.g. kleertjes (*clothing*).

When used to refer to time, the Dutch diminutive form can indicate whether the person in question found it pleasant or not: *een uur*tje *kletsen* (*chatting for a "little" hour.*) The diminutive can, however, also be used pejoratively: *Hij was weer eens het "mannetje"*. (*He acted as if he was the "little" man.*)

All diminutives (even lexicalised ones like "meisje" *(girl)*) have neuter gender and take neuter concords: "*dit* kleine meisje", not "*deze* kleine meisje".

### Pronouns and determiners

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Pronouns and determiners")]

There are two series of personal pronouns, subject and objects pronouns. The forms on the right-hand sides within each column are the unemphatic forms; those not normally written are given in brackets. Only *ons* and *u* do not have an unemphatic form. The distinction between emphatic and unemphatic pronouns is very important in Dutch.[[132]](#cite_note-Donaldson-145): 67 [Emphatic pronouns](/wiki/Emphatic_pronoun "Emphatic pronoun") in English use the [reflexive pronoun](/wiki/Reflexive_pronoun "Reflexive pronoun") form, but are used to emphasise the subject, not to indicate a direct or indirect object. For example, "I gave (to) myself the money" is reflexive but "I myself gave the money (to someone else) " is emphatic.

| [person](/wiki/Grammatical_person "Grammatical person") | [subject](/wiki/Subject_(grammar) "Subject (grammar)") | [object](/wiki/Object_(grammar) "Object (grammar)") |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1st person singular | *ik – ('k)* | *mij – me* |
| 2nd person singular, informal | *jij – je* | *jou – je* |
| 2nd person singular, formal | *u* | *u* |
| 3rd person singular, masculine | *hij – (ie)* | *hem – ('m)* |
| 3rd person singular, feminine | *zij – ze* | *haar – ('r, d'r)* |
| 3rd person singular, neuter | *het – ('t)* | *het – ('t)* |
|  | | |
| 1st person plural | *wij – we* | *ons* |
| 2nd person plural, informal | *jullie – je* | *jullie – je* |
| 2nd person plural, formal | *u* | *u* |
| 3rd person plural, for a person | *zij – ze* | *hun, hen – ze* |
| 3rd person plural, for an object | *zij – ze* | *die – ze* |

Like English, Dutch has generalised the dative over the accusative case for all pronouns, e.g. NL '*me*', '*je*', EN 'me', 'you', vs. DE '*mich*'/'*mir*' '*dich*'/'*dir*'. There is one exception: the standard language prescribes that in the third person plural, *hen* is to be used for the direct object, and *hun* for the indirect object. This distinction was artificially introduced in the 17th century by grammarians, and is largely ignored in spoken language and not well understood by Dutch speakers. Consequently, the third person plural forms *hun* and *hen* are interchangeable in normal usage, with *hun* being more common. The shared unstressed form *ze* is also often used as both direct and indirect objects and is a useful avoidance strategy when people are unsure which form to use.[[133]](#cite_note-146)

Dutch also shares with English the presence of h- pronouns, e.g. NL *hij*, *hem*, *haar*, *hen*, *hun* and EN *he*, *him*, *her* vs. DE *er*, *ihn*, *ihr*, *ihnen*.

### Compounds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Compounds")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Bord_Hemelwaterinfiltratiegebied.JPG/250px-Bord_Hemelwaterinfiltratiegebied.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bord_Hemelwaterinfiltratiegebied.JPG)

The 27-letter compound *hemelwaterinfiltratiegebied* ([rainwater infiltration area](/wiki/Permeable_paving "Permeable paving")) on a traffic sign in [Zwolle](/wiki/Zwolle "Zwolle"), Netherlands

Like most Germanic languages, Dutch forms noun [compounds](/wiki/Compound_(linguistics) "Compound (linguistics)"), where the first noun modifies the category given by the second (*hondenhok* = doghouse). Unlike English, where newer compounds or combinations of longer nouns are often written in open form with separating spaces, Dutch (like the other Germanic languages) either uses the closed form without spaces (*boomhut* = tree house) or inserts a hyphen (*VVD-coryfee* = outstanding member of the [VVD](/wiki/VVD "VVD"), a political party). Like German, Dutch allows arbitrarily long compounds, but the longer they get, the less frequent they tend to be.

The longest serious entry in the [Van Dale](/wiki/Van_Dale "Van Dale") dictionary is [*wapenstilstandsonderhandeling*](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/7/7d/Nl-BE-Wapenstilstandsonderhandeling.oga/Nl-BE-Wapenstilstandsonderhandeling.oga.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-BE-Wapenstilstandsonderhandeling.oga "File:Nl-BE-Wapenstilstandsonderhandeling.oga") (ceasefire negotiation). Leafing through the articles of association (Statuten) one may come across a 30-letter [*vertegenwoordigingsbevoegdheid*](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/9/9d/Nl-BE-Vertegenwoordigingsbevoegdheid.oga/Nl-BE-Vertegenwoordigingsbevoegdheid.oga.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-BE-Vertegenwoordigingsbevoegdheid.oga "File:Nl-BE-Vertegenwoordigingsbevoegdheid.oga") (authorisation of representation). An even longer word cropping up in official documents is ["ziektekostenverzekeringsmaatschappij"](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/7/7a/Nl-ziektekostenverzekeringsmaatschappij.ogg/Nl-ziektekostenverzekeringsmaatschappij.ogg.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-ziektekostenverzekeringsmaatschappij.ogg "File:Nl-ziektekostenverzekeringsmaatschappij.ogg")(health insurance company) though the shorter *zorgverzekeraar* (health insurer) is more common.

Notwithstanding official spelling rules, some Dutch-speaking people, like some Scandinavians and German speakers, nowadays tend to write the parts of a compound separately, a practice sometimes dubbed *de Engelse ziekte* (the English disease).[[134]](#cite_note-147)

## Vocabulary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Vocabulary")]

Dutch vocabulary is predominantly Germanic in origin, with loanwords accounting for 20%.[[135]](#cite_note-148) The main foreign influence on Dutch vocabulary since the 12th century and culminating in the [French period](/wiki/French_period "French period") has been French and (northern) [Oïl languages](/wiki/Langues_d%27o%C3%AFl "Langues d'oïl"), accounting for an estimated 6.8% of all words, or more than a third of all loanwords. [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), which was spoken in the southern Low Countries for centuries and then played a major role as the language of science and religion, follows with 6.1%. High German and Low German were influential until the mid-20th century and account for 2.7%, but they are mostly unrecognisable since many have been "Dutchified": German *Fremdling* → Dutch *vreemdeling*. Dutch has borrowed words from English since the mid-19th century, as a consequence of the increasing power and influence of Britain and the United States. English loanwords are about 1.5%, but continue to increase.[[136]](#cite_note-149) Many English loanwords become less visible over time as they are either gradually replaced by [calques](/wiki/Calque "Calque") (*skyscraper* became Dutch *wolkenkrabber*) or [neologisms](/wiki/Neologism "Neologism") (*bucket list* became *loodjeslijst*). Conversely, Dutch contributed many loanwords to English, accounting for 1.3% of its lexicon.[[137]](#cite_note-150)

The main Dutch dictionary is the *[Van Dale groot woordenboek der Nederlandse taal](/wiki/Van_Dale "Van Dale")*, which contains some 268,826 [headwords](/wiki/Headword "Headword").[[138]](#cite_note-151) In the field of [linguistics](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics"), the 45,000-page *[Woordenboek der Nederlandsche Taal](/wiki/Woordenboek_der_Nederlandsche_Taal "Woordenboek der Nederlandsche Taal")* is also widely used. That scholarly endeavour took 147 years to complete and contains all recorded Dutch words from the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages") onward.

## Spelling and writing system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Spelling and writing system")]

Main articles: [Dutch orthography](/wiki/Dutch_orthography "Dutch orthography"), [History of Dutch orthography](/wiki/History_of_Dutch_orthography "History of Dutch orthography"), [Dutch six-dot Braille](/wiki/Dutch_six-dot_Braille "Dutch six-dot Braille"), and [Dutch eight-dot Braille](/wiki/Dutch_eight-dot_Braille "Dutch eight-dot Braille")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Amsterdamse_tram_-_De_Red_Crosser_-_from_Flickr_2838709455_cropped_lijnbus.jpg/250px-Amsterdamse_tram_-_De_Red_Crosser_-_from_Flickr_2838709455_cropped_lijnbus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Amsterdamse_tram_-_De_Red_Crosser_-_from_Flickr_2838709455_cropped_lijnbus.jpg)

Dutch uses the [digraph](/wiki/Digraph_(orthography) "Digraph (orthography)") ⟨[ij](/wiki/IJ_(digraph) "IJ (digraph)")⟩ as a single letter and it can be seen in several variations. Here, a marking saying *[lijnbus](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/lijnbus#Dutch "wikt:lijnbus")* ("line/route" + "bus"; the tram lane also serves as bus road).

Dutch is written using the [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script"). Dutch uses one additional character beyond the standard alphabet, the [digraph](/wiki/Digraph_(orthography) "Digraph (orthography)") ⟨[ij](/wiki/IJ_(digraph) "IJ (digraph)")⟩. It has a relatively high proportion of doubled letters, both vowels and consonants, due to the formation of compound words and also to the spelling devices for distinguishing the many vowel sounds in the Dutch language. An example of five consecutive doubled letters is the word *voorraaddoos* ([food storage container](/wiki/Food_storage_container "Food storage container")). The [diaeresis](/wiki/Diaeresis_(diacritic) "Diaeresis (diacritic)") (Dutch: *trema*) is used to mark vowels that are pronounced separately when involving a pre- or suffix, and a [hyphen](/wiki/Hyphen "Hyphen") is used when the problem occurs in compound words, e.g. *beïnvloed* (influenced), *de zeeën* (the seas) but *zee-eend* (scoter; lit. sea duck). Generally, other [diacritical marks](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic") occur only in loanwords. However, the [acute accent](/wiki/Acute_accent "Acute accent") can also be used for emphasis or to differentiate between two forms, and its most common use is to differentiate between the indefinite [article](/wiki/Article_(grammar) "Article (grammar)") *een* /ən/ "a, an" and the numeral *één* /eːn/ "one".

Since the 1980s, the Dutch Language Union has been given the mandate to review and make recommendations on the official spelling of Dutch. Spelling reforms undertaken by the union occurred in 1995 and 2005. In the Netherlands, the official spelling is currently given legal basis by the Spelling Act of September 15, 2005.[[n]](#cite_note-152)[[o]](#cite_note-153) The Spelling Act gives the Committee of Ministers of the Dutch Language Union the authority to determine the spelling of Dutch by ministerial decision. In addition, the law requires that this spelling be followed "at the governmental bodies, at educational institutions funded from the public purse, as well as at the exams for which legal requirements have been established". In other cases, it is recommended, but it is not mandatory to follow the official spelling. The Decree on the Spelling Regulations 2005 of 2006 contains the annexed spelling rules decided by the Committee of Ministers on April 25, 2005.[[p]](#cite_note-154)[[q]](#cite_note-155) In Flanders, the same spelling rules are currently applied by the Decree of the Flemish Government Establishing the Rules of the Official Spelling and Grammar of the Dutch language of June 30, 2006.[[r]](#cite_note-156)

The *[Woordenlijst Nederlandse taal](/wiki/Word_list_of_the_Dutch_language "Word list of the Dutch language")*, more commonly known as *het groene boekje* (i.e. "the green booklet", because of its colour), is the authoritative orthographic word list (without definitions) of the Dutch Language Union; a version with definitions can be had as *Het Groene Woordenboek*; both are published by [Sdu](/wiki/Sdu_(publishing_company) "Sdu (publishing company)").

## Example text

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Example text")]

Dutch pronunciation

Article 1 of the [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights") in Dutch:

:   *Alle mensen worden vrij en gelijk in waardigheid en rechten geboren. Zij zijn begiftigd met verstand en geweten, en behoren zich jegens elkander in een geest van broederschap te gedragen.*[[139]](#cite_note-157)

Article 1 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights in English:

:   All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. They are endowed with reason and conscience and should act towards one another in a spirit of brotherhood.[[140]](#cite_note-158)
