## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dutch_language&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** Afrikaans is a daughter language of Dutch; see [Booij 1999](#CITEREFBooij1999), p. 2, [Jansen, Schreuder & Neijt 2007](#CITEREFJansenSchreuderNeijt2007), p. 5, [Mennen, Levelt & Gerrits 2006](#CITEREFMennenLeveltGerrits2006), p. 1, [Booij 2003](#CITEREFBooij2003), p. 4, [Hiskens, Auer & Kerswill 2005](#CITEREFHiskensAuerKerswill2005), p. 19, [Heeringa & de Wet 2007](#CITEREFHeeringade_Wet2007), pp. 1, 3, 5.
     
   Afrikaans was historically called Cape Dutch; see [Deumert & Vandenbussche 2003](#CITEREFDeumertVandenbussche2003), p. 16, [Conradie 2005](#CITEREFConradie2005), p. 208, [Sebba 1997](#CITEREFSebba1997), p. 160, [Langer & Davies 2005](#CITEREFLangerDavies2005), p. 144, [Deumert 2002](#CITEREFDeumert2002), p. 3, [Berdichevsky 2004](#CITEREFBerdichevsky2004), p. 130.
     
   Afrikaans is rooted in 17th-century dialects of Dutch; see [Holm 1989](#CITEREFHolm1989), p. 338, [Geerts & Clyne 1992](#CITEREFGeertsClyne1992), p. 71, [Mesthrie 1995](#CITEREFMesthrie1995), p. 214, [Niesler, Louw & Roux 2005](#CITEREFNieslerLouwRoux2005), p. 459.
     
   Afrikaans is variously described as a creole, a partially creolised language, or a deviant variety of Dutch and has a vastly simplified grammar compared to Dutch; see [Sebba 2007](#CITEREFSebba2007), p. 116.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** It has the widest geographical and racial distribution of all official languages of South Africa; see [Webb 2003](#CITEREFWebb2003), pp. 7, 8, [Berdichevsky 2004](#CITEREFBerdichevsky2004), p. 131. It has by far the largest geographical distribution; see [Alant 2004](#CITEREFAlant2004), p. 45.  
   It is widely spoken and understood as a second or third language; see [Deumert & Vandenbussche 2003](#CITEREFDeumertVandenbussche2003), p. 16, [Kamwangamalu 2004](#CITEREFKamwangamalu2004), p. 207, [Myers-Scotton 2006](#CITEREFMyers-Scotton2006), p. 389, [Simpson 2008](#CITEREFSimpson2008), p. 324, [Palmer 2001](#CITEREFPalmer2001), p. 141, [Webb 2002](#CITEREFWebb2002), p. 74, [Herriman & Burnaby 1996](#CITEREFHerrimanBurnaby1996), p. 18, [Page & Sonnenburg 2003](#CITEREFPageSonnenburg2003), p. 7, [Brook Napier 2007](#CITEREFBrook_Napier2007), pp. 69, 71.  
   An estimated 40 percent of South Africans have at least a basic level of communication in Afrikaans; see [Webb 2003](#CITEREFWebb2003), p. 7 [McLean & McCormick 1996](#CITEREFMcLeanMcCormick1996), p. 333. Afrikaans is a lingua franca of Namibia; see [Deumert 2004](#CITEREFDeumert2004), p. 1, [Adegbija 1994](#CITEREFAdegbija1994), p. 26, [Batibo 2005](#CITEREFBatibo2005), p. 79, [Donaldson 1993](#CITEREFDonaldson1993), p. xiii, [Deumert & Vandenbussche 2003](#CITEREFDeumertVandenbussche2003), p. 16, [Baker & Prys Jones 1998](#CITEREFBakerPrys_Jones1998), p. 364, [Domínguez & López 1995](#CITEREFDomínguezLópez1995), p. 399, [Page & Sonnenburg 2003](#CITEREFPageSonnenburg2003), p. 8, [CIA 2010](#CITEREFCIA2010).  
   While the number of total speakers of Afrikaans is unknown, estimates range between 15 and 23 million. Afrikaans has 16.3 million speakers; see [de Swaan 2001](#CITEREFde_Swaan2001), p. 216. Afrikaans has a total of 16 million speakers; see [Machan 2009](#CITEREFMachan2009), p. 174. About 9 million people speak Afrikaans as a second or third language; see [Alant 2004](#CITEREFAlant2004), p. 45, [Proost 2006](#CITEREFProost2006), p. 402. Afrikaans has over 5 million native speakers and 15 million second language speakers; see [Réguer 2004](#CITEREFRéguer2004), p. 20. Afrikaans has about 6 million native and 16 million second language speakers; see [Domínguez & López 1995](#CITEREFDomínguezLópez1995), p. 340. In South Africa, over 23 million people speak Afrikaans to some degree, of which a third are first-language speakers; see [Page & Sonnenburg 2003](#CITEREFPageSonnenburg2003), p. 7. L2 "Black Afrikaans" is spoken, with different degrees of fluency, by an estimated 15 million; see [Stell 2008](#CITEREFStell2008), p. 1.  
   Dutch and Afrikaans share mutual intelligibility; see [Gooskens 2007](#CITEREFGooskens2007), p. 453, [Holm 1989](#CITEREFHolm1989), p. 338, [Baker & Prys Jones 1998](#CITEREFBakerPrys_Jones1998), p. 302, [Egil Breivik & Håkon Jahr 1987](#CITEREFEgil_BreivikHåkon_Jahr1987), p. 232. For written mutual intelligibility; see [Sebba 2007](#CITEREFSebba2007), p. 116, [Sebba 1997](#CITEREFSebba1997), p. 161.  
   It is easier for Dutch speakers to understand Afrikaans than the other way around; see [Gooskens 2007](#CITEREFGooskens2007), p. 454.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-14)** 410,000 in the United States, 159,000 in Canada, 47,000 in Australia; see [Simpson 2009](#CITEREFSimpson2009), p. 307. Between 200,000 and 400,000 in US alone; see [McGoldrick, Giordano & Garcia-Preto 2005](#CITEREFMcGoldrickGiordanoGarcia-Preto2005), p. 536.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-16)** In France, a historical dialect called [French Flemish](/wiki/French_Flemish "French Flemish") is spoken. There are about 80,000 Dutch speakers in France; see [Simpson 2009](#CITEREFSimpson2009), p. 307. In French Flanders, only a remnant of 20,000 Flemish-speakers remain; see [Berdichevsky 2004](#CITEREFBerdichevsky2004), p. 90. French Flemish is spoken in the north-west of France by an estimated population of 20,000 daily speakers and 40,000 occasional speakers; see [European Commission 2010](#CITEREFEuropean_Commission2010).  
   A dialect continuum exists between Dutch and German through the [Kleverlandish](/wiki/Kleverlandish "Kleverlandish") and [Limburgish](/wiki/Limburgish_language "Limburgish language") dialects.  
   In 1941, 400,000 Indonesians spoke Dutch, and Dutch exerted a major influence on Indonesian; see [Sneddon 2003](#CITEREFSneddon2003), p. 161. In 1941, about 0.5% of the inland population had a reasonable knowledge of Dutch; see [Maier 2005](#CITEREFMaier2005), p. 12. At the beginning of World War II, about one million Asians had an active command of Dutch, while an additional half million had a passive knowledge; see [Jones 2008](#CITEREFJones2008), p. xxxi. Many older Indonesians speak Dutch as a second language; see [Thomson 2003](#CITEREFThomson2003), p. 80. Some of the ethnic Chinese in Indonesia speak Dutch amongst each other; see [Tan 2008](#CITEREFTan2008), pp. 62–64, [Erdentuğ & Colombijn 2002](#CITEREFErdentuğColombijn2002), p. 104. Dutch is spoken by "smaller groups of speakers" in Indonesia; see [Bussmann 2002](#CITEREFBussmann2002), p. 83. Some younger Indonesians learn Dutch as a foreign language because their parents and grandparents may speak it and because in some circles, Dutch is regarded as the language of the elite; see [Vos 2001](#CITEREFVos2001), p. 91. At present, only educated people of the oldest generation, in addition to specialists who require knowledge of the language, can speak Dutch fluently; see [Ammon et al. 2006](#CITEREFAmmonDittmarMattheierTrudgill2006), p. 2017. Around 6.4% of present-day Indonesian vocabulary can be traced back to Dutch words, see [Tadmor 2009](#CITEREFTadmor2009), p. 698.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-17)** Dutch and English are the closest relatives of German; see [Abraham 2006](#CITEREFAbraham2006), p. 124. Dutch is the closest relative of German; see [Czepluch & Abraham 2004](#CITEREFCzepluchAbraham2004), p. 13. Dutch and English are closely related; see [Ingram 1989](#CITEREFIngram1989), p. 494, [Todd 2004](#CITEREFTodd2004), p. 37, [Kager 1989](#CITEREFKager1989), p. 105, [Hogg 2002](#CITEREFHogg2002), p. 134, [De Bot, Lowie & Verspoor 2005](#CITEREFDe_BotLowieVerspoor2005), pp. 130, 166, [Weissenborn & Höhle 2001](#CITEREFWeissenbornHöhle2001), p. 209, [Crisma & Longobarde 2009](#CITEREFCrismaLongobarde2009), p. 250. Dutch and English are very closely related languages; see [Fitzpatrick 2007](#CITEREFFitzpatrick2007), p. 188. Dutch is, after Frisian, the closest relative of English; see [Mallory & Adams 2006](#CITEREFMalloryAdams2006), p. 23, [Classe 2000](#CITEREFClasse2000), p. 390, [Hogg 2002](#CITEREFHogg2002), p. 3, [Denning, Kessler & Leben 2007](#CITEREFDenningKesslerLeben2007), p. 22. English is most closely related to Dutch; see [Lightfoot 1999](#CITEREFLightfoot1999), p. 22, and more so than to German; see [Sonnenschein 2008](#CITEREFSonnenschein2008), p. 100, [Kennedy Wyld 2009](#CITEREFKennedy_Wyld2009), p. 190.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-18)** Dutch is traditionally described as morphologically between English and German, but syntactically closer to German; see [Clyne 2003](#CITEREFClyne2003), p. 133. Dutch has been positioned to be between English and German; see [Putnam 2011](#CITEREFPutnam2011), p. 108, [Bussmann 2002](#CITEREFBussmann2002), p. 83, [Müller 1995](#CITEREFMüller1995), p. 121, [Onysko & Michel 2010](#CITEREFOnyskoMichel2010), p. 210. Typologically, Dutch takes a position between German and English, a similar word order to that of German, grammatical gender, and a largely Germanic vocabulary with many cognates to German words. It is morphologically close to English, and the case system and subjunctive have largely fallen out of use; see [Swan & Smith 2001](#CITEREFSwanSmith2001), p. 6.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-19)** Dutch shares with English its simplified morphology and the abandonment of the [grammatical case system](/wiki/Grammatical_case "Grammatical case"); see [Booij 1999](#CITEREFBooij1999), p. 1, [Simpson 2009](#CITEREFSimpson2009), p. 309. In contrast to German, case markings have become vestigial in English and Dutch; see [Hogg 2002](#CITEREFHogg2002), p. 134, [Abraham 2006](#CITEREFAbraham2006), p. 118, [Bussmann 2002](#CITEREFBussmann2002), p. 83, [Swan & Smith 2001](#CITEREFSwanSmith2001), p. 6. The umlaut in Dutch and English matured to a much lesser extent than in German; see [Simpson 2009](#CITEREFSimpson2009), p. 307, [Lass 1994](#CITEREFLass1994), p. 70, [Deprez 1997](#CITEREFDeprez1997), p. 251.
8. **[^](#cite_ref-20)** Dutch has effectively two genders; see [Booij 1999](#CITEREFBooij1999), p. 1, [Simpson 2009](#CITEREFSimpson2009), p. 309, [De Vogelaer 2009](#CITEREFDe_Vogelaer2009), p. 71. Grammatical gender has little grammatical consequences in Dutch; see [Bussmann 2002](#CITEREFBussmann2002), p. 84
9. **[^](#cite_ref-22)** [Simpson 2009](#CITEREFSimpson2009), p. 307, [Booij 1999](#CITEREFBooij1999), p. 1 Dutch and German do not have a strict SVO order as in English; see [Hogg 2002](#CITEREFHogg2002), pp. 87, 134. In contrast to English, which has SVO as the underlying word order, for Dutch and German this is SV1OV2 or (in subordinate clauses) SOV; see [Ingram 1989](#CITEREFIngram1989), p. 495, [Jordens & Lalleman 1988](#CITEREFJordensLalleman1988), pp. 149, 150, 177. Dutch has almost the same word order as German; see [Swan & Smith 2001](#CITEREFSwanSmith2001), p. 6.
10. **[^](#cite_ref-23)** Dutch vocabulary has more Germanic words than English and more Romance words than German; see [Simpson 2009](#CITEREFSimpson2009), p. 309, [Swan & Smith 2001](#CITEREFSwanSmith2001), p. 17. Dutch vocabulary is mostly Germanic; see [Swan & Smith 2001](#CITEREFSwanSmith2001), p. 6. Dutch has the most similar vocabulary to English; see [Mallory & Adams 2006](#CITEREFMalloryAdams2006), p. 1.
11. **[^](#cite_ref-61)** Friedrich Maurer uses the term *[Istvaeonic](/wiki/Istvaeones "Istvaeones")* instead of Franconian; see Friedrich Maurer (1942), *Nordgermanen und Alemannen: Studien zur germanischen und frühdeutschen Sprachgeschichte, Stammes- und Volkskunde*, Bern: Verlag Francke.
12. **[^](#cite_ref-notconstitution_77-0)** The Dutch language does have the status of official language in the Netherlands, together with the Dutch Sign Language and West-Frisian (in Friesland). However, it is not legally enshrined in the [Dutch constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Netherlands "Constitution of the Netherlands"), which is uncommon in the European Union.[[63]](#cite_note-74)[[64]](#cite_note-75)[[65]](#cite_note-denederlandsegrondwet.nl-2018-76) There is almost no legally defined status of Dutch stipulated anywhere in legislation.[[65]](#cite_note-denederlandsegrondwet.nl-2018-76) A long series of parliamentary and public discussions in the 2010s on the question whether to enshrine Dutch as the official language of the Netherlands came to nothing, and the proposal was withdrawn again by the government in February 2018.[[65]](#cite_note-denederlandsegrondwet.nl-2018-76)
